# Code Review

Review all changes on the current branch against main.

## Process

### 1. Gather Changes

```bash
git diff main --name-only
```

If no changes, inform the user and stop.

For each changed file, read the FULL file (not just the diff) to understand context. Also run:

```bash
git diff main
```

### 2. Review Against Guidelines

#### Code Reads Like Prose
- Descriptive function and variable names — no abbreviations that require mental decoding
- Linear data flows — can you read top-to-bottom without jumping around?
- Efficient line count — no bloat, no unnecessary abstractions
- DRY — is anything duplicated that shouldn't be?
- Single responsibility — each function does ONE thing, each class has ONE purpose
- Testable — could you write a test for this without mocking half the system?

#### Make Invalid States Unrepresentable
- Constraints encoded in types, not runtime checks
- No scattered validation for the same invariant
- `std::optional`, enums, and constructors used to enforce validity

#### Trace the Data
- Where does each value come from? What constraints does it carry?
- Are there redundant conversions or unnecessary intermediates?
- Does every downstream consumer get what it expects?

#### Always Make It Better
- Did we touch code without improving it?
- Are there obvious improvements just outside the diff boundary? (note in suggestions, don't block)

#### Project Conventions (from CLAUDE.md)
- No docstrings unless asked
- No fallbacks — code works or fails loudly
- No dead code, no commented-out experiments
- No default arguments
- Leading underscores for class data members
- PascalCase functions, snake_case locals, kCamelCase constants
- "latest" not "last" for most recent values
- Parameter passing: pointer for mutation, const ref for read-only, const value for scalars

### 3. Check For Slop
- Comments that explain "what" instead of "why"
- Defensive checks for conditions that can't happen
- Unnecessary try/catch or error handling beyond system boundaries
- Verbose logging that doesn't match existing patterns
- Style inconsistencies with surrounding code

### 4. Present Results

```
## Review Summary

**Assessment**: [APPROVE / REQUEST CHANGES]

### Positive Findings
- What was done well

### Required Changes
- Issues that must be fixed (file:line references)

### Suggestions
- Optional improvements

### Convention Violations
- Any CLAUDE.md or guideline violations
```

Be specific with file:line references. Prioritize correctness and readability over micro-optimizations. If approved, suggest running `/validate-smol` next.


ARGUMENTS: We should take another pass and reviewing all the code using our guidelines

---

Fix the 6 required, and see how you can apply the suggestions as well. we always want to make the code better, even if we didn't touch a certain part with the current change

3. Files and Code Sections:

   **`src/control/rc_pwm.h`** (NEW, most recently modified)
   - Central PWM constants + normalize helpers; eliminates duplication between rc_input.cpp and aux_resolver.cpp
   ```cpp
   #pragma once
   #include <cstdint>
   #include "control/control_types.h"
   
   inline constexpr uint16_t kRcPwmMin = 988;
   inline constexpr uint16_t kRcPwmMax = 2012;
   inline constexpr uint16_t kRcPwmCenter = 1500;
   inline constexpr uint16_t kRcSwitchLowThreshold = 1300;
   inline constexpr uint16_t kRcSwitchHighThreshold = 1700;
   
   inline Bidirectional NormalizePwmBidirectional(const uint16_t pwm) {
     if (pwm == 0) return Bidirectional(0.0f);
     const float centered = static_cast<float>(pwm) - static_cast<float>(kRcPwmCenter);
     const float half_range = static_cast<float>(kRcPwmMax - kRcPwmCenter);
     return Bidirectional(centered / half_range);
   }
   
   inline Unidirectional NormalizePwmUnidirectional(const uint16_t pwm) {
     if (pwm == 0) return Unidirectional(0.0f);
     const float normalized = (static_cast<float>(pwm) - static_cast<float>(kRcPwmMin)) /
                              static_cast<float>(kRcPwmMax - kRcPwmMin);
     return Unidirectional(normalized);
   }
   ```

   **`src/control/aux_resolver.cpp`** (most recently modified)
   - Uses shared `NormalizePwmUnidirectional(...).Value()` 
   - kGcs case falls through to neutral idle
   ```cpp
   #include "control/aux_resolver.h"
   #include "control/rc_pwm.h"
   #include "core/skill_command.h"
   
   static bool DoesPilotClaim(const AuxChannelPolicy& p, const RcInput& rc) { ... }
   
   AuxOutput AuxResolver::ArbitrateAuxChannels(...) const {
     // ...
     if (DoesPilotClaim(p, rc)) {
       out.values[i] = NormalizePwmUnidirectional(rc.channels[p.rc_value_ch]).Value();
       out.sources[i] = ControlSource::kPilot;
       continue;
     }
     // kGcs case merged with kNone/kPilot → neutral idle
   }
   ```

   **`src/control/failsafe_event.h`**
   - `FailsafeEvent::Now(kind, trigger)` factory captures Clock timestamp
   - New `FailsafeEventKindName(kind)` function fixes silent kNone→"T1_EXIT" mapping bug
   ```cpp
   struct FailsafeEvent {
     FailsafeEventKind kind = FailsafeEventKind::kNone;
     FailsafeTrigger trigger = FailsafeTrigger::kNone;
     uint32_t timestamp_ms = 0;
     static FailsafeEvent Now(const FailsafeEventKind k, const FailsafeTrigger t) {
       return {.kind = k, .trigger = t, .timestamp_ms = Clock::ElapsedMs()};
     }
   };
   inline constexpr const char* FailsafeEventKindName(const FailsafeEventKind kind) {
     switch (kind) {
       case FailsafeEventKind::kNone: return "NONE";
       case FailsafeEventKind::kTier1Entered: return "T1_ENTER";
       case FailsafeEventKind::kTier1Exited: return "T1_EXIT";
     }
     __builtin_unreachable();
   }
   ```

                                            const uint8_t primary_count) {
     const size_t capacity = kMaxServoChannels - primary_count;
     const size_t count = std::min(aux.values.size(), capacity);
     for (size_t i = 0; i < count; i++) {
       pwm_out[primary_count + i] = ToPwm(Unidirectional(aux.values[i]));

# Backlog Mode

You are in **Backlog Mode** - documenting bugs and improvements WITHOUT implementing them.

**Reference skills based on issue domain:**
- API issues: Read `.claude/skills/api-design-patterns/SKILL.md`
- Frontend components: Read `.claude/skills/react-patterns/SKILL.md`
- Error handling: Read `.claude/skills/error-handler/SKILL.md`
- UX improvements: Read `.claude/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md`

## Available Tools & When to Use Them

### Codebase Exploration
Use **Task tool with Explore subagent** for:
- Finding root causes of bugs
- Understanding current implementation
- Locating affected files and components
```
Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Very thorough: find all code related to [X]")
```

### External Research
Use **Exa search** (`mcp__exa__exa_search`) for:
- Checking if others encountered the same bug
- Finding known fixes or workarounds
- Researching UI/UX best practices for similar features
```
mcp__exa__exa_search(query="[framework] [issue description] fix")
```

Use **Ref documentation** (`mcp__Ref__*`) for:
- Checking official framework/library docs
- Verifying correct API usage
- Finding recommended patterns
```
mcp__Ref__ref_search_documentation(query="[library] [feature] documentation")
```

### UI/UX Verification
Use **Playwright** (`mcp__playwright__*`) for UX issues:
- Navigate to the affected page
- Take screenshot of current state (for BACKLOG entry)
- Verify the issue exists as user described
- Document "before" state
```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate(url="http://localhost:3000/path")
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot(filename="backlog-ux-xxx-before.png")
```

### Complex Analysis
Use **Sequential thinking** (`mcp__sequential-thinking__sequentialthinking`) for:
- Multi-step root cause analysis
- Weighing multiple potential causes
- Structuring complex investigations

---

## Parallel Execution Strategy

You CAN run multiple subagents AND tools in parallel by making multiple tool calls in a single message. Use this for efficiency when tasks are **independent**.

### Multiple Subagents in Parallel

**When to use:**
- Bug/feature spans frontend + backend (separate concerns)
- Investigation touches multiple packages
- Need both code exploration AND documentation research
- Complex issue benefits from divide-and-conquer

**Example - Bug spanning layers:**
```
[Single message, multiple Task calls - all run simultaneously:]

Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Very thorough: find tRPC handlers for [feature]")
Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Very thorough: find React components rendering [feature]")
Task(subagent_type="documentation-researcher", prompt="Research [framework] best practices for [pattern]")
```

**Example - Multi-package investigation:**
```
Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Search packages/trpc for [issue]")
Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Search packages/features for [issue]")
Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Search apps/web for [issue]")
```

### Subagents + Tools in Parallel

**Example - UX issue (all at once):**
```
Task(subagent_type="Explore", prompt="Find component code for [element]")
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate(url="http://localhost:3000/path")
mcp__exa__exa_search(query="UX best practices [pattern]")
```

### When to Stay Sequential

- Results from first search inform what to search next
- Need to confirm issue exists before deep diving
- Small codebase where one Explore is sufficient
- Risk of overlapping/duplicated search areas

**Example - Sequential dependency:**
```
1. First: Explore → discovers it's a Prisma schema issue
2. Then: Ref search → "Prisma relations documentation"
   (wouldn't know to search Prisma without step 1)
```

### Rule of Thumb
| Situation | Approach |
|-----------|----------|
| Know what to search for | **Parallel** - maximize speed |
| Need to discover what to search | **Sequential** - learn first |
| Large codebase, clear separation | **Parallel subagents** - divide and conquer |
| Small codebase or focused issue | **Single subagent** - avoid overhead |

---

## Initial Setup

1. **Check for arguments**: If user passed `The functions in cpp files should be defined in the same order as they are in the header files.`, use it as the first item description
2. **Locate BACKLOG.md** by searching:
   - `_private/docs/BACKLOG.md`
   - `_private/BACKLOG.md`
   - `BACKLOG.md`
3. **If not found**: Ensure `_private/` is in `.gitignore`, then create `_private/docs/BACKLOG.md` with this template:
   ```markdown
   # Project Backlog

   Issues and improvements tracked for this project.

   ---

   ## Bugs

   (No bugs reported yet)

   ---

   ## Enhancements

   (No enhancements reported yet)

   ---

   ## UX Improvements

   (No UX improvements reported yet)

   ---

   ## Completed

   Items moved here after being fixed/implemented.

   ---

   ## Statistics
   - **Total Open**: 0
   - **Bugs**: 0
   - **Enhancements**: 0
   - **UX**: 0
   - **Last Updated**: YYYY-MM-DD
   ```

---

## Workflow Loop

For each item, execute these steps IN ORDER:

### Step 1: Receive
- Accept ONE item at a time
- If user provides multiple, say: "Let's start with the first one. What is it?"
- If `The functions in cpp files should be defined in the same order as they are in the header files.` provided, use that as the item

### Step 2: Classify
Determine type based on description:
| Type | Criteria |
|------|----------|
| **BUG** | Something broken, error, unexpected behavior |
| **ENH** | New feature, capability, or functional improvement |
| **UX** | Visual, usability, or interface improvement |

State your classification: "This sounds like a **[TYPE]**."

### Step 3: Check Duplicates
- Read BACKLOG.md
- Search for similar existing entries
- If potential duplicate found: "This might be related to [ID]. Is this the same issue or different?"

### Step 4: Investigate (Use Appropriate Tools)

**For ALL types:**
- Use **Explore subagent** to find relevant code

**For BUGs additionally:**
- Use **Exa** to search if others hit this issue
- Check for known fixes or workarounds

**For ENH additionally:**
- Use **Ref** to check official docs for best practices
- Use **Exa** to research how others implement similar features

**For UX additionally:**
- Use **Playwright** to navigate to the page and take a screenshot
- Verify the issue exists as described
- Save screenshot as `backlog-ux-XXX-current.png` for documentation

### Step 5: Analyze
Use sequential thinking if complex. Summarize:
- What you found in codebase
- What external research revealed (if any)
- Root cause (BUG) or approach (ENH/UX)
- All affected files with full paths
- Suggested priority (P1/P2/P3)

### Step 6: Preview Entry
Show the complete BACKLOG entry to user:
```
Here's the proposed entry:

### [TYPE-XXX] Title
- **Reported**: date
- **Status**: Open
...

Does this look accurate? Any changes?
```

### Step 7: Document
After user confirms (or says "looks good", "yes", "ok"):
- Add entry to appropriate section in BACKLOG.md
- Update Statistics section
- Confirm: "Added as **[TYPE-XXX]** to BACKLOG.md"

### Step 8: Next Item
Ask: "Next item? (or 'done' to exit backlog mode)"
- If user provides another item → Go to Step 1
- If user says "done", "exit", "that's all" → End with summary of items added

---

## Critical Rules

1. **NO IMPLEMENTATION** - Only analyze and document
2. **NO CODE CHANGES** - Only read/explore, write only to BACKLOG.md
3. **ALWAYS PREVIEW** - Never add without user confirmation
4. **ONE AT A TIME** - Don't batch multiple items
5. **USE SUBAGENTS** - For thorough codebase exploration
6. **RESEARCH WHEN RELEVANT** - Use Exa/Ref for external context
7. **SCREENSHOT UX ISSUES** - Visual documentation helps
8. **INCREMENT IDs** - Check highest existing ID, add 1

---

## Priority Guidelines

| Priority | Use When |
|----------|----------|
| **P1 (Critical)** | Blocks core functionality, security risk, data loss, affects all users |
| **P2 (Medium)** | Significant but has workaround, important feature request |
| **P3 (Low)** | Minor issue, cosmetic, nice-to-have |

---

## Templates

### BUG-XXX
```markdown
### [BUG-XXX] Title
- **Reported**: YYYY-MM-DD
- **Status**: Open
- **Priority**: P1/P2/P3
- **Description**: What's broken
- **Steps to Reproduce**:
  1. First step
  2. Second step
- **Expected**: What should happen
- **Actual**: What happens instead
- **Root Cause Analysis**:
  - Finding from exploration
  - Why this occurs
- **External Research**: (if relevant - similar issues found, known fixes)
- **Affected Files**:
  - `path/to/file.ts:lineNum` - description
- **Proposed Fix**: High-level approach (NOT code)
```

```markdown
### [ENH-XXX] Title
- **Reported**: YYYY-MM-DD
- **Status**: Open
- **Priority**: P1/P2/P3
- **Description**: What to add/improve
- **User Impact**: Who benefits, how
- **Current State**: How it works now
- **Proposed Solution**: High-level approach
- **Best Practices**: (from research if relevant)
- **Scope**: Small (hours) / Medium (day) / Large (days+)
- **Affected Files**:
  - `path/to/file.ts` - what changes needed
- **Dependencies**: Blockers or prerequisites
```

### UX-XXX
```markdown
### [UX-XXX] Title
- **Reported**: YYYY-MM-DD
- **Status**: Open
- **Priority**: P1/P2/P3
- **Description**: UX issue or improvement
- **Current Behavior**: How it looks/works now
- **Proposed Behavior**: How it should look/work
- **Screenshot**: `.playwright-mcp/backlog-ux-XXX-current.png` (if captured)
- **UX Best Practices**: (from research if relevant)
- **Affected Components**:
  - `path/to/Component.tsx` - changes needed
- **Implementation Notes**: Technical considerations
```

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-review

# Code Review

Review all changes on the current branch against main.

## Process

### 1. Gather Changes

```bash
git diff main --name-only
```

If no changes, inform the user and stop.

For each changed file, read the FULL file (not just the diff) to understand context. Also run:

```bash
git diff main
```

### 2. Review Against Guidelines

#### Code Reads Like Prose
- Descriptive function and variable names — no abbreviations that require mental decoding
- Linear data flows — can you read top-to-bottom without jumping around?
- Efficient line count — no bloat, no unnecessary abstractions
- DRY — is anything duplicated that shouldn't be?
- Single responsibility — each function does ONE thing, each class has ONE purpose
- Testable — could you write a test for this without mocking half the system?

#### Make Invalid States Unrepresentable
- Constraints encoded in types, not runtime checks
- No scattered validation for the same invariant
- `std::optional`, enums, and constructors used to enforce validity

#### Trace the Data
- Where does each value come from? What constraints does it carry?
- Are there redundant conversions or unnecessary intermediates?
- Does every downstream consumer get what it expects?

#### Always Make It Better
- Did we touch code without improving it?
- Are there obvious improvements just outside the diff boundary? (note in suggestions, don't block)

#### Project Conventions (from CLAUDE.md)
- No docstrings unless asked
- No fallbacks — code works or fails loudly
- No dead code, no commented-out experiments
- No default arguments
- Leading underscores for class data members
- PascalCase functions, snake_case locals, kCamelCase constants
- "latest" not "last" for most recent values
- Parameter passing: pointer for mutation, const ref for read-only, const value for scalars

### 3. Check For Slop
- Comments that explain "what" instead of "why"
- Defensive checks for conditions that can't happen
- Unnecessary try/catch or error handling beyond system boundaries
- Verbose logging that doesn't match existing patterns
- Style inconsistencies with surrounding code

### 4. Present Results

```
## Review Summary

**Assessment**: [APPROVE / REQUEST CHANGES]

### Positive Findings
- What was done well

### Required Changes
- Issues that must be fixed (file:line references)

### Suggestions
- Optional improvements

### Convention Violations
- Any CLAUDE.md or guideline violations
```

Be specific with file:line references. Prioritize correctness and readability over micro-optimizations. If approved, suggest running `/validate-smol` next.


ARGUMENTS: Let's go thorugh the code one more time before we commit

---

"p" would be a lot less confusing if we used the actual data type instead of "auto":
for (const auto& p : table.channels) {
    assert((p.rc_claim_ch < 0 || p.rc_value_ch >= 0) && "pilot claim requires value channel");
  }

---

I think we should tend towards always specifying the data type unless it's EXTREMELY obvious. I would rather be changing this TO "auto" rather than FROM "auto"

---

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/Documents/Github/smol/.claude/skills/validate-smol

# Validate smol

Run all automated checks for the smol flight controller. Activate `~/.venvs/smol` before every command.

**CRITICAL**: Capture the FULL output of every command. Never re-run a command just to see which tests failed — read the output you already have. Use `2>&1` to merge stderr, and save long outputs to a file if needed.

## Checks

Run these in order. If a check fails, stop and report — do not continue to later checks.

### 1. Regenerate and format

Regenerate all generated files and run the formatter. This ensures we never commit stale generated code or unformatted source.

```bash
python3 protocol/generate.py && just format && just check-format && just check-codegen
```

Both checks must pass. Generated files (`src/protocol/`) are excluded from formatting — the generator owns their style.

### 2. Native unit tests

```bash
just test-native 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-native.txt
```

Read the FULL output to find failures. Do NOT re-run just to see which tests failed.

Report pass/fail count from the summary line (e.g., "118 test cases: 118 succeeded").

Known flaky: `test_smol_matches_full_after_full_sequence` (BUG-006, FullEkf nondeterminism). If ONLY this test fails, re-run once. If it fails twice, report it.

### 3. Thread sanitizer

```bash
just test-tsan 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-tsan.txt
```

Catches data races in SharedState, ring buffer, and cross-thread communication. Requires `setarch -R` for ASLR compatibility on Linux 6.x.

### 4. Elixir GCS tests

```bash
just test-gcs 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-gcs.txt
```

### 5. Native build

```bash
just build-native
```

### 6. RP2350B build (CMake)

```bash
just build-fc3
```

This catches missing source files in `CMakeLists.txt` and RP2350B-specific compilation errors that native builds miss.

### 7. Replay validation

```bash
just test-replay 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-replay.txt
```

This builds `native_full_ekf`, replays reference flight data through both EKFs, and checks RMS error bounds vs truth. Requires steps 4-5 to have passed.

## Report

```
## Validation Report

### Regenerate + format
[PASS — tree clean / PASS — N files updated / FAIL — details]

### Native tests
[PASS — N test cases: N succeeded / FAIL — details]

### Thread sanitizer
[PASS / FAIL — details]

### GCS tests
[PASS — N tests: N passed / FAIL — details]

### Native build
[PASS / FAIL]

### RP2350B build
[PASS / FAIL — details if failed]

### Replay validation
[PASS — N passed / FAIL — details]

### Summary
[READY TO SHIP / NEEDS FIXES — list what failed]
```

## Next Steps

- All pass: `/ship` to commit
- Failures: fix and re-run `/validate-smol`

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/ship-pr

# Ship PR

Create a feature branch from the current branch, commit all changes, push, and open a PR.

## Pre-flight

Verify we have changes to ship:

```bash
git status
git diff --stat
git diff --cached --stat
```

If there are no changes (staged or unstaged, tracked or untracked), stop and inform the user.

## Gather Context

```bash
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline -5
```

The current branch becomes the PR's **base branch** (the branch to merge into). If already on a feature branch, ask the user if they want to PR into the branch they branched from instead.

## Steps

### 1. Determine branch name and commit message

If the user provided arguments, use them to derive:
- **Branch name**: kebab-case, prefixed with `feat/`, `fix/`, or `refactor/` based on the change type. Max 50 chars.
- **Commit message**: conventional commit format from the same description.

If no arguments provided, analyze the diff to determine both:
- Read `git diff` and `git diff --cached` and any untracked files
- Classify the change (feat, fix, refactor, docs, test, chore)
- Draft a branch name and commit message

**Show the user the proposed branch name and commit message before proceeding.** Wait for approval.

### 2. Create branch and commit

```bash
git checkout -b <branch-name>
```

Stage all relevant files. **Do NOT stage:**
- `.env` files or credentials
- Large binary files unless they're clearly intentional (e.g., `.uf2` firmware)

```bash
git add <specific files>
git commit -m "<message>

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>"
```

Use a HEREDOC for multi-line commit messages.

### 3. Push

```bash
git push -u origin <branch-name>
```

### 4. Create PR

```bash
gh pr create --base <base-branch> --title "<title>" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
<1-3 bullet points describing the changes>

## Test plan
<How the changes were validated>


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
```

The PR body should summarize what changed and how it was tested. Reference any validation output if `/validate-smol` or similar was run.

### 5. Report

```
PR created: <url>
Branch: <branch-name> → <base-branch>
```

## Safety Rules

- **Never force push**
- **Never push to main/master directly** — always create a branch
- **Always show the branch name and commit message before creating them**
- **If the user is already on a feature branch with commits**, ask whether to create a new branch or use the current one
- Stage files explicitly — never use `git add -A` or `git add .`

Run the helper script to fetch all PR comments since the latest commit:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/fetch_pr_comments.sh
```

Then process the output:

1. Parse the issue comments, review comments, and review summaries from the script output.
2. Itemize the list of comments in descending order, with the highest priority comments first.
3. For each comment, include its author and assess if it is a valid comment. If it is valid, provide a detailed description of how you would address the comment.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   This session continued from a compacted prior session working on the `feat/mission-graph-phase-1-fast-loop` branch of the smol flight controller. The user drove multiple iterative review/cleanup cycles:
   - Enforce "no single-letter variable" convention across PR code (with loop-counter exemption for `i`/`j`)
   - Explain and enforce multi-line designated-init convention (trailing comma → clang-format preserves multi-line)
   - Codify post-edit clang-format rule in CLAUDE.md
   - Delete redundant `kMaxAuxChannels` alias; use `protocol::kAuxChannelMax` directly at 10 call sites
   - Full /cp-review pass against all guidelines
   - Codify "prefer explicit types over auto" convention
   - /validate-smol full validation
   - /ship-pr — create PR against main, include flight* fixtures, delete scheduled_tasks.lock
   - Two rounds of /cp-pr-fetcher — address Gemini bot review comments

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - smol flight controller: C++20, Eigen, googletest, dual-build (PlatformIO native + CMake FC3v2)
   - Mission graph architecture, Emissare pattern
   - Protocol codegen: Jinja2 → C++ + Elixir (protocol hash 0x0BF52B)
   - clang-format trailing-comma as "do not collapse" marker (bi-modal: comma → multi-line, no comma → one-line)
   - `__builtin_unreachable()` for exhaustive switch defaults
   - Aggregate types + designated init (C++20)
   - `protocol::kAuxChannelMax = 4`, `protocol::kEventsPerTickMax = 4`
   - `kRcChannelCount = 16` (hal/sensor_types.h)
   - Static Clock singleton with `SetElapsedUsForTest`
   - `ExpiringValue<T>::Set(value)` / `Get(max_age_sec)` reads Clock internally
   - SafetyMonitor (pure verdict) vs FailsafeResponder (override state + loiter)
   - Three-tier aux arbitration (pilot > GCS > skill/default)
   - FOR_EACH_PARAM X-macro generating `ParamIndex::k##EnumSuffix`
   - Pre-commit hooks (black, flake8, clang-format)

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **CLAUDE.md** — Added 3 new conventions:
     ```
     - Prefer explicit types over `auto`. Default to spelling the type; reach for
       `auto` only when the type is genuinely unnamable (lambdas, some template
       return types) or when writing it produces more noise than signal (verbose
       iterator types). If you're unsure, spell it. `auto` combined with a short
       variable name forces the reader to jump to the definition — don't hide
       both at once.
     - Struct creation with designated initializers (2+ fields): always multi-line
       with a trailing comma on the final field. clang-format uses the trailing
       comma as a "do not collapse" marker:
       ```cpp
       return {
           .kind = kind,
           .trigger = trigger,
           .timestamp_ms = Clock::ElapsedMs(),
       };
       ```
       Single-field designated inits may stay one-line (`X{.field = value}`).
     - Run `clang-format -i <file>` on every `.h`/`.cpp` you modify before
       reporting a task complete.
     ```

   - **src/control/aux_resolver.cpp** — Central bound-check fix for Gemini round 1 #1+#2:
     ```cpp
     static bool DoesPilotClaim(const AuxChannelPolicy& policy, const RcInput& rc) {
       if (policy.rc_value_ch < 0 || policy.rc_value_ch >= kRcChannelCount) {
         return false;
       }
       if (policy.rc_claim_ch >= kRcChannelCount) {
         return false;
       }
       if (policy.rc_claim_ch < 0) {
         return rc.channels[policy.rc_value_ch] != 0;
       }
       return rc.channels[policy.rc_claim_ch] >= kRcSwitchHighThreshold;
     }
     ```
     Also uses `protocol::kAuxChannelMax` directly (alias deleted earlier). Uses `i`/`j` loop counters per exemption. Includes `hal/sensor_types.h` for `kRcChannelCount`.

   - **src/control/aux_channel_policy.cpp** — Boot-time assert tightened:
     ```cpp
     for (const AuxChannelPolicy& p : table.channels) {
       assert(p.rc_value_ch < kRcChannelCount && "rc_value_ch out of range");
       assert(p.rc_claim_ch < kRcChannelCount && "rc_claim_ch out of range");
       assert((p.rc_claim_ch < 0 || p.rc_value_ch >= 0) && "pilot claim requires value channel");
     }
     ```
     Note: `auto` changed to `AuxChannelPolicy` to make the short name `p` readable.

   - **src/control/failsafe_responder.cpp** — Gemini round 1 #3 (orphaned params) + round 2 #2 (dropped Update):
     ```cpp
     FailsafeResponse resp = {};
     // TODO(phase-5): consume _loiter.Update output to drive the cruise pipeline
     // (speed/course/alt commands). Also gate Capture on nav validity — today
     // Capture at zero-nav is harmless because the loiter output is ignored, but
     // Phase 5 will need a `nav_valid` input to skip entry when EKF is down.
     if (!_loiter.IsActive()) {
       const dubins::FollowerParams follower = {
           .line_gain = params::Get<ParamIndex::kNavLineGain>(),
           .orbit_gain = params::Get<ParamIndex::kNavOrbitGain>(),
           .max_correction_deg = params::Get<ParamIndex::kNavMaxCorrDeg>(),
       };
       const dubins::TurnDir turn_dir =
           params::Get<ParamIndex::kLoiterDir>() >= 0 ? dubins::TurnDir::kCw : dubins::TurnDir::kCcw;
       _loiter.Capture(nav.north_m, nav.east_m, -nav.down_m, params::Get<ParamIndex::kTurnRadiusM>(),
                       turn_dir, follower, params::Get<ParamIndex::kAirspeedTrim>());
       resp.event = FailsafeEvent::Now(FailsafeEventKind::kTier1Entered, verdict.trigger);
     }

     // Phase 1 placeholder: shallow-bank orbit. Phase 5 replaces with loiter
     // follower output driving the cruise pipeline.
     resp.control = ControlOutput{...};
     ```

   - **src/mixing/servo_mixer.cpp** — Gemini round 2 #1 (buffer overflow guard):
     ```cpp
     uint8_t ServoMixer::AppendAuxPassthrough(const AuxOutput& aux, uint16_t* pwm_out,
                                              const uint8_t control_channel_count) {
       assert(control_channel_count <= kMaxServoChannels);
       if (control_channel_count >= kMaxServoChannels) {
         return 0;
       }
       const size_t capacity = kMaxServoChannels - control_channel_count;
       const size_t count = std::min(aux.values.size(), capacity);
       ...
     }
     ```
     Added `#include <cassert>`.

   - **src/control/cascade_controller.cpp** — Gemini round 1 #4 (style fix, lines 232-236):
     ```cpp
     return AttitudeRateCommand{
         .roll_rps = roll_rate_rps,
         .pitch_rps = pitch_rate_rps,
         .yaw_rps = yaw_rate_rps,
     };
     ```

   - **src/control/aux_output.h** — Simplified after alias deletion:
     ```cpp
     #include "control/aux_command.h"
     #include "protocol/fc_config.h"

     struct AuxOutput {
       std::array<float, protocol::kAuxChannelMax> values = {};
       std::array<ControlSource, protocol::kAuxChannelMax> sources = {};
     };
     ```

   - **src/core/flight_loop.cpp** — `auto airspeed_ias` → `std::optional<float> airspeed_ias`.

   - **test/test_safety_monitor/test_safety_monitor.cpp** — 7× `auto` → explicit `SafetyVerdict` / `SafetyObservation`.

   - **test/test_platform/test_platform.cpp** — 6× `auto` → `std::optional<float>` in ExpiringValue tests.

   - **docs/BACKLOG.md** — Added ENH-073: "Order .cpp function definitions to match .h declaration order" (~60% mismatch rate, Medium scope).

4. Errors and fixes:

   - **Overcorrection on loop counters**: User said "I think we went overboard on the indices inside for loops. For example, here, 'i' would have been fine..." User confirmed revert for `channel_idx`→`i` and `defaults_idx`→`j` in aux_resolver.cpp.

   - **Bulk replace broke test_aux_resolver formatting**: When replacing `t` → `policy`, my Edit with `replace_all=true` matched partial string and broke formatting. Fixed by rewriting file with Write.

   - **auto vs short variable name**: User pushback — "'p' would be a lot less confusing if we used the actual data type instead of 'auto'". Fix: changed `const auto& p` to `const AuxChannelPolicy& p`. User generalized: "tend towards always specifying the data type unless it's EXTREMELY obvious. I would rather be changing this TO 'auto' rather than FROM 'auto'". Codified in CLAUDE.md.

   - **Trailing comma misunderstanding**: User added trailing comma manually, then removed it. Asked "how do we get these return values to be formatted to use multiple lines?" I demonstrated clang-format behavior: trailing comma = multi-line, no comma = one-line.

   - **Missing flight* files + scheduled_tasks.lock in initial push**: User: "You should include the flight* files. you should delete that scheduled_tasks file." Did both in commit ee0d8e72.

   - **Initial grep missed existing params**: When checking for `kLoiterDir`, `kNavLineGain` etc., first grep with `k` prefix missed them because source uses `LoiterDir`, `NavLineGain` (X-macro prepends `k`). Second grep found them in param_store.h. Wired into failsafe_responder.cpp.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Established clean principle: "auto is fine when surrounding code makes the type obvious; short names are fine when the type is spelled out. Don't hide both at once."
   - Demonstrated clang-format trailing-comma mechanism via test file.
   - Closed cross-cutting convention: always run clang-format after edits (since user's editor only formats on save, not for Claude's edits).
   - Deleted orphan `kMaxAuxChannels` alias — used `protocol::kAuxChannelMax` directly at 10 sites.
   - Validated Phase 1 ship-readiness via /validate-smol (all 7 checks pass).
   - Addressed 6 Gemini bot comments across two rounds.

6. All user messages:
   - "I'm looking in @src/control/aux_resolver.cpp, and you used a one-letter variable name. That violates our conventions, except when the variable is explicitly used for a math variable, in like in the EKF. You need to check for other violations in this PR: for (int i = 0; i < kMaxAuxChannels; i++) { const AuxChannelPolicy& p = policy.channels[i];"
   - "I think we went overboard on the indices inside for loops. For example, here, 'i' would have been find because we're clearing iterating over all the channels. Look back over all the changes you just made, and find opportunities for abbreviations or single letters. Show me all of the variable changes, and I will tell you which ones to implement"
   - "Revert 1 and 2."
   - "How do we get these return values to be formatted to use multiple lines, i.e., add a comma after the last item? static FailsafeEvent Now(..."
   - "No, I added that comma myself. I just removed it. Show me how clang format would fix it. I never want to be viewing files that haven't been formatted correctly and automatically"
   - "Oh, okay, so it's up to you to use a trailing comma? Then we need to add that to @CLAUDE.md. I always want struct creations to use multiple lines.    - smol flight controller (C++20, Eigen, googletest, dual-build: PlatformIO native + CMake FC3v2)
   - Mission graph architecture (7 phases: 0–6)

   - `__builtin_unreachable()` integrity requires upstream validation (parse-don't-validate pattern)
   - "No fallbacks" convention — code either works or fails loudly via assert
   - Protocol codegen: Jinja2 → C++ + Elixir, hash-verified
   - clang-format trailing-comma marker for multi-line preservation

     FailsafeResponse FailsafeResponder::ComputeFailsafeResponse(
         const SafetyVerdict& verdict, const ControlOutput& passthrough,
         const std::optional<StateEstimate>& nav, const float dt_s) {
       const FailsafeTier prev = _active_tier;
       _active_tier = verdict.tier;
       if (verdict.tier == FailsafeTier::kNone) { ... }
       FailsafeResponse resp = {};
       if (prev == FailsafeTier::kNone) {
         resp.event = FailsafeEvent::Now(FailsafeEventKind::kTier1Entered, verdict.trigger);
       }
       if (nav && !_loiter.IsActive()) { _loiter.Capture(nav->north_m, ...); }
       resp.control = ControlOutput{ ... };  // Phase 1 placeholder shallow-bank orbit
       return resp;
     }
     ```

     TEST_F(FailsafeResponderTest, Tier1WithNullNavEmitsEntryAndPlaceholderOutput) {
       FailsafeResponder responder;
       SafetyVerdict verdict{FailsafeTier::kTier1, FailsafeTrigger::kEkfDivergence};
       FailsafeResponse resp =
           responder.ComputeFailsafeResponse(verdict, ControlOutput{}, std::nullopt, 0.01f);
       EXPECT_EQ(resp.event.kind, FailsafeEventKind::kTier1Entered);
       EXPECT_EQ(resp.event.trigger, FailsafeTrigger::kEkfDivergence);
       EXPECT_GT(resp.control.throttle.Value(), 0.1f);
     }
     ```

---

Gemini had one comment:
src/platform/mailbox_state.h (78-81)

medium

Draining the queue by popping every element involves a memcpy for each item. While efficient for the default kCapacity = 2, this loop could become a performance bottleneck if the capacity is increased for large types T. Consider adding a constraint on kCapacity or documenting that this primitive is optimized for very small buffers.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - Help test ADR-A (`docs/adr/adr-a-sharedstate-skillcommand-DRAFT.md`) on the RP2350B board. The ADR compared two cross-loop primitive candidates for `SharedState<SkillCommand>`: Variant A (`SeqlockState<T>`) and Variant B (`MailboxState<T, N>`), both pending on-target measurement.
   - Clean up spike scaffolding after the decision was made.
   - Commit the acceptance.
   - Check PR #74 review comments via `/cp-pr-fetcher` skill (twice) and address them.
   - After user's pivotal pushback: remove the prematurely-created `MailboxState<T, N>` class entirely because it had no production consumer, and let the primitive land at its first use site in Phase 2 instead.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - smol flight controller (C++20, dual-build: PlatformIO native + CMake RP2350B FC3v2)
   - ADR-A: decision between SeqlockState (Variant A) and MailboxState/drain-to-latest-SPSC (Variant B) for large-T cross-loop channels
   - `std::atomic<T>::is_always_lock_free` being `false` on Cortex-M33 for T > 16 bytes → silent libatomic mutex fallback
   - `SkillCommand` — 552 bytes on target
   - `FilterConfig` — 96 bytes (latent same-class bug found during spike)
   - Drain-to-latest pattern over `etl::queue_spsc_atomic` (reader loops `while (q.pop(x)) {}`)
   - RP2350B BOOTSEL USB mass storage flashing (no picotool needed)
   - Python serial capture via pyserial
   - Gemini code review bot (medium-priority comments)
   - CLAUDE.md convention conflict with HYP-10: pointer out-params vs return-value preference
   - YAGNI / no premature abstraction (pivotal project principle)

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **`docs/adr/adr-a-sharedstate-skillcommand-DRAFT.md` → `docs/adr/adr-a-sharedstate-skillcommand.md`** (renamed, heavily rewritten)
     - Purpose: canonical decision record for cross-loop primitive choice.
     - Changes: renamed (dropped DRAFT), status ACCEPTED, filled measurement table with real RP2350B data, wrote decision section (Variant B), wrote consequences. After user pushback, rewrote Consequences section to stop naming a class as if it existed:
     ```
     **Status:** ACCEPTED — Variant B (drain-to-latest over `etl::queue_spsc_atomic`). 
     The primitive is introduced at its first use site in Phase 2, not as a standalone library class.
     ```
     - Final Consequences section describes the mechanism as a three-line drain loop at call site:
     ```cpp
     T cmd;
     while (skill_queue.pop(cmd)) { /* loop body runs 0..N-1 times; cmd ends holding latest */ }
     ```

   - **`src/platform/mailbox_state.h`** (created then deleted)
     - Originally accepted as the class implementing Variant B. Went through multiple review rounds: added `static_assert(kCapacity <= 4)` after Gemini flagged O(N) drain cost. Then simplified `ReadLatest` to pop directly into output. Then deleted entirely after user said "Why does MailboxState still exist as a class? It's not being used anywhere."

   - **`test/test_mailbox_state/test_mailbox_state.cpp`** (created then deleted)
     - Had 5 tests (WriteReadLatestSingle, ReadLatestDrainsAll, WriteWhenFullDrops, EmptyReadLatestDoesNotAdvance, ConcurrentWriterReaderEventuallyConsistent). All passed. Deleted when parent class deleted.

   - **`docs/architecture/hypotheses.md`** (line 130, HYP-7 Position)
     - Initially updated to name `MailboxState<T, N>`. Then reverted after user pushback to pattern-level language:
     ```
     **Position.** Continuous channels crossing the fast/slow loop boundary use `SharedState<T>` when T is small and lock-free-capable, or a drain-to-latest SPSC wrapper when T is too large for lock-free atomic snapshot (see ADR-A for the decision and measurement — the wrapper lands at its first use site). Discrete events that must not drop use `etl::queue_spsc_atomic<Msg, N>` directly. Anything else requires architectural justification.
     ```

   - **`docs/architecture/ownership.md`** (line 65, firmware-lead Owns)
     - Added MailboxState, then reverted. Final: removed the mention entirely, kept "`SharedState<T>` and SPSC queue semantics".

   - **`.claude/agents/firmware-lead.md`** (line 3, description)
     - Added MailboxState<T, N>, then reverted back to original list `(SharedState<T>, etl::queue_spsc_atomic)`.

   - **`docs/adr/README.md`** (line 70)
     - Status cell progressed: "Draft" → "Accepted — `MailboxState<T, N>`" → "Accepted — drain-to-latest SPSC; implementation lands at Phase 2 first use site".

   - **`CMakeLists.txt`** (removed 40-line BUILD_SPIKE_ADR_A block)

   - **`platformio.ini`** (temporarily added `-<main_rp2350b_spike_adr_a.cpp>` to native + native_sil build_src_filter, then reverted when spike file was deleted — net zero change)

   - **`src/main_rp2350b_spike_adr_a.cpp`** (added `#include <pico/stdlib.h>` for stdio_init_all, then whole file deleted)

   - **`bench/sharedstate_skillcommand.cpp`** (deleted — whole bench/ directory)

   - **`src/platform/seqlock_state.h`** (deleted — rejected Variant A)

   - **`test/test_seqlock_mailbox/`** (deleted — replaced by trimmed test_mailbox_state/, which was itself later deleted)

   - **`/tmp/adr_a_capture.py`** (Python serial capture script for RP2350B USB CDC)

   - **`/tmp/adr-a-spike.txt`** (captured board output):
     ```
     ADR-A SPIKE v1
     sizeof SkillCommand=552 SeqlockState=556 MailboxState=1680
     std::atomic<SkillCommand>::is_always_lock_free=false
     VARIANT_A reader P50=0 P95=1000 P99=1000 MAX=3000 retries_total=0 retries_max=0
     VARIANT_B reader P50=0 P95=1000 P99=1000 MAX=3000 drops=0
     DONE
     ```

   - **`~/.claude/projects/-home-ubuntu-Documents-Github-smol/memory/feedback_no_class_without_caller.md`** (new feedback memory)
     - Captured the YAGNI lesson from user's pushback: "Don't commit a library / helper class until production code uses it. When an ADR decides on a primitive, the ADR is the deliverable. The class ships at the first use site."

   - **`~/.claude/projects/-home-ubuntu-Documents-Github-smol/memory/MEMORY.md`** (added index line under Feedback section):
     ```
     - [No class without a caller](feedback_no_class_without_caller.md) — ADR captures the decision; the class lands at its first use site. Don't ship scaffolding ahead of consumers — review cycles on ghost code waste attention.
     ```

4. Errors and fixes:

   - **Native test build failure (FreeRTOS.h not found)**: `main_rp2350b_spike_adr_a.cpp` wasn't excluded from native env's `build_src_filter`. Fixed by adding `-<main_rp2350b_spike_adr_a.cpp>` to both `env:native` and `env:native_sil` sections. Later reverted when file was deleted.

   - **RP2350B build failure (stdio_init_all not declared)**: Missing `#include <pico/stdlib.h>`. Fixed by adding the include in src/main_rp2350b_spike_adr_a.cpp.

   - **Gemini first review (5 comments)**: Applied all 5 as reasonable improvements.

   - **Gemini second review conflict**: Gemini suggested pointer → reference for `ReadLatest(T*)`, citing HYP-10. But CLAUDE.md explicitly says "Output/in-out data: `Type*` pointer". Surfaced the conflict to user rather than silently applying. User's response was actually about a deeper issue: the class shouldn't exist at all.

   - **Critical user feedback (YAGNI)**: *"Why does MailboxState still exist as a class? It's not being used anywhere. And the whole file is written poorly. We have captured the decision in the adr-a document. There's no reason to prematurely create this class."* 
     - I acknowledged I was wrong: I had committed a library class with no production consumer and then spent four review cycles polishing cosmetic details (capacity bounds, pointer vs reference, stack frames, doc wording) of code nobody used.
     - Fix: deleted the class + test + all doc ripples that named the class. Kept the ADR as the single decision artifact. Saved as a feedback memory.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Validated ADR-A's load-bearing hypothesis on target: `is_always_lock_free=false` confirmed, seqlock retries=0 on RP2350B (vs 150-342 on host — an OS-preemption artifact).
   - Decision: Variant B wins by ADR's stated simpler-option tie-break (both pass correctness gates at P99=1µs).
   - Surfaced latent bug: `SharedState<FilterConfig>` (sizeof=96) already uses libatomic mutex fallback; migration paired with static_assert is in ADR-A follow-up list.
   - Course-corrected from over-engineering: accepted that the class should not exist, deleted it, let Phase 2 author introduce the call-site pattern when it has a real caller.

   > "★ Insight — The signal I should have caught earlier: when every subsequent review comment on a newly-landed file is about cosmetic API details (capacity bounds → pointer vs reference → stack frames → doc wording), that's the reviewer system doing what it's designed to do against code that has no real consumer to push back with real constraints. Review bots fill the feedback vacuum with low-stakes opinions because there's nothing higher-stakes to find."
   
   Closing suggestion to user:

# Ask the smol agents

Invoke the `orchestrator` agent with the user's question. Let it route.

The user's question is in `Are we ready to start planning phase 2? We have 3 ADRs (ADR-A accepted, ADR-B and ADR-C still draft). The goal: before we spend time writing a Phase 2 plan file, I want to know whether we have enough one-way-door decisions committed to avoid re-opening them mid-implementation.`. Pass it verbatim.

## What to do

1. Invoke the orchestrator via the Task tool:
   - `subagent_type`: `orchestrator`
   - `description`: "Route smol question"
   - `prompt`: the user's full question from `Are we ready to start planning phase 2? We have 3 ADRs (ADR-A accepted, ADR-B and ADR-C still draft). Are they sufficient to cover Phase 2, or do we need to answer more questions first? Please route to relevant leads (mission-graph-lead, systems-architect, safety-director) for parallel perspectives. The goal: before we spend time writing a Phase 2 plan file, I want to know whether we have enough one-way-door decisions committed to avoid re-opening them mid-implementation.`, verbatim. Include any context from the current conversation that would help the orchestrator classify (e.g., "the user is currently editing `src/foo.cpp`").

2. Present the orchestrator's output to the user without modification. Do not paraphrase, summarize, or condense.

## What not to do

- Do not answer the question yourself. That is the orchestrator's and the domain agents' job.
- Do not pick an agent manually and bypass the orchestrator. If the user wanted a specific agent, they would have used `/smol-review` or invoked the agent by name.
- Do not edit the orchestrator's output. Especially: do not soften or resolve disagreement maps. Disagreement is the high-value output.

## If the orchestrator asks a clarifying question

Pass it through to the user and wait for their reply before spawning anything further.

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans — one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## No Placeholders

Every step must contain the actual content an engineer needs. These are **plan failures** — never write them:
- "TBD", "TODO", "implement later", "fill in details"
- "Add appropriate error handling" / "add validation" / "handle edge cases"
- "Write tests for the above" (without actual test code)
- "Similar to Task N" (repeat the code — the engineer may be reading tasks out of order)
- Steps that describe what to do without showing how (code blocks required for code steps)
- References to types, functions, or methods not defined in any task

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in every step — if a step changes code, show the code
- Exact commands with expected output
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Self-Review

After writing the complete plan, look at the spec with fresh eyes and check the plan against it. This is a checklist you run yourself — not a subagent dispatch.

**1. Spec coverage:** Skim each section/requirement in the spec. Can you point to a task that implements it? List any gaps.

**2. Placeholder scan:** Search your plan for red flags — any of the patterns from the "No Placeholders" section above. Fix them.

**3. Type consistency:** Do the types, method signatures, and property names you used in later tasks match what you defined in earlier tasks? A function called `clearLayers()` in Task 3 but `clearFullLayers()` in Task 7 is a bug.

If you find issues, fix them inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on. If you find a spec requirement with no task, add the task.

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review

# Slop Command

Check the diff against main and remove all AI-generated slop introduced in this branch.

## What is "Slop"?

AI-generated code often has tells that make it look unnatural:

### 1. Over-Commenting
```typescript
// BAD: AI slop
// Get the user from the database
const user = await db.getUser(id); // Fetch user by ID

// GOOD: Self-documenting, no comment needed
const user = await db.getUser(id);
```

### 2. Defensive Overkill
```typescript
// BAD: Unnecessary defensive checks
function processUser(user: User) {
  if (!user) {
    throw new Error('User is required');
  }
  if (!user.id) {
    throw new Error('User must have an id');
  }
  // ... when caller already validates
}

// GOOD: Trust internal callers
function processUser(user: User) {
  // Just do the work - caller handles validation
}
```

### 3. Type Escapes
```typescript
// BAD: Casting to escape type issues
const data = response as any;
const items = (result as unknown as Item[]);

// GOOD: Fix the actual type
const data: ResponseType = response;
```

### 4. Inconsistent Style
- Different naming conventions than the file uses
- Different error handling patterns
- Different import styles
- Added JSDoc when file doesn't use it (or vice versa)

### 5. Unnecessary Try/Catch
```typescript
// BAD: Wrapping things that don't throw
try {
  const x = a + b;
  return x;
} catch (error) {
  console.error('Failed to add numbers', error);
  throw error;
}

// GOOD: Just do it
return a + b;
```

### 6. Verbose Logging
```typescript
// BAD: Logging everything
console.log('Starting process...');
console.log('User ID:', userId);
console.log('Processing...');
console.log('Done!');

// GOOD: Log meaningful events only (or nothing for simple ops)
```

## Workflow

### 1. Get the Diff

```bash
git diff main...HEAD
```

Identify all files changed in this branch.

### 2. For Each Changed File

1. Read the FULL file (not just the diff) to understand existing patterns
2. Compare new code against existing style:
   - Comment density
   - Error handling approach
   - Naming conventions
   - Type annotation style
   - Import organization

### 3. Identify Slop

Look for code that:
- Has MORE comments than surrounding code
- Has MORE defensive checks than similar code paths
- Uses `any` or type assertions
- Has different formatting/style than the file
- Adds try/catch around non-throwing code
- Adds logging that doesn't match file patterns

### 4. Fix It

Remove or adjust:
- Delete unnecessary comments
- Remove redundant validation (if callers are trusted)
- Fix types properly instead of casting
- Match existing code style
- Remove pointless try/catch
- Remove excessive logging

**Use Edit tool to make changes.**

### 5. Report

End with a **1-3 sentence summary only**:

```
Removed 4 unnecessary comments, 2 redundant null checks, and fixed
1 `any` cast in UserService.ts. Deleted try/catch wrapper in
processPayment() since the caller handles errors.
```

## What NOT to Remove

- Comments that explain non-obvious business logic
- Validation at system boundaries (user input, external APIs)
- Error handling that matches the file's existing patterns
- Logging that matches existing patterns
- Type assertions that are genuinely necessary

## Example

**Before (AI slop):**
```typescript
/**
 * Fetches user data from the database
 * @param userId - The ID of the user to fetch
 * @returns The user object or null if not found
 */
async function getUser(userId: string): Promise<User | null> {
  // Validate the user ID
  if (!userId) {
    throw new Error('userId is required');
  }

  try {
    // Fetch the user from database
    const user = await db.users.findOne({ id: userId });

    // Return the user
    return user as any as User;
  } catch (error) {
    // Log the error
    console.error('Failed to fetch user:', error);
    throw error;
  }
}
```

**After (cleaned):**
```typescript
async function getUser(userId: string): Promise<User | null> {
  return db.users.findOne({ id: userId });
}
```

**Report:** "Removed JSDoc (file doesn't use it), deleted redundant userId validation (TypeScript enforces it), removed try/catch (caller handles errors), fixed `any` cast by using proper return type."

## Notes

- Run AFTER implementation, before /review
- Focuses on style consistency, not functionality
- Be conservative - when unsure, leave it
- Check git diff after to verify changes make sense

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **CLAUDE.md** — Added 3 new conventions:
     ```
     - Prefer explicit types over `auto`. Default to spelling the type; reach for
       `auto` only when the type is genuinely unnamable (lambdas, some template
       return types) or when writing it produces more noise than signal (verbose
       iterator types). If you're unsure, spell it. `auto` combined with a short
       variable name forces the reader to jump to the definition — don't hide
       both at once.
     - Struct creation with designated initializers (2+ fields): always multi-line
       with a trailing comma on the final field. clang-format uses the trailing
       comma as a "do not collapse" marker:
       ```cpp
       return {
           .kind = kind,
           .trigger = trigger,
           .timestamp_ms = Clock::ElapsedMs(),
       };
       ```
       Single-field designated inits may stay one-line (`X{.field = value}`).
     - Run `clang-format -i <file>` on every `.h`/`.cpp` you modify before
       reporting a task complete.
     ```

   - **src/control/aux_resolver.cpp** — Central bound-check fix for Gemini round 1 #1+#2:
     ```cpp
     static bool DoesPilotClaim(const AuxChannelPolicy& policy, const RcInput& rc) {
       if (policy.rc_value_ch < 0 || policy.rc_value_ch >= kRcChannelCount) {
         return false;
       }
       if (policy.rc_claim_ch >= kRcChannelCount) {
         return false;
       }
       if (policy.rc_claim_ch < 0) {
         return rc.channels[policy.rc_value_ch] != 0;
       }
       return rc.channels[policy.rc_claim_ch] >= kRcSwitchHighThreshold;
     }
     ```
     Also uses `protocol::kAuxChannelMax` directly (alias deleted earlier). Uses `i`/`j` loop counters per exemption. Includes `hal/sensor_types.h` for `kRcChannelCount`.

   - **src/control/aux_channel_policy.cpp** — Boot-time assert tightened:
     ```cpp
     for (const AuxChannelPolicy& p : table.channels) {
       assert(p.rc_value_ch < kRcChannelCount && "rc_value_ch out of range");
       assert(p.rc_claim_ch < kRcChannelCount && "rc_claim_ch out of range");
       assert((p.rc_claim_ch < 0 || p.rc_value_ch >= 0) && "pilot claim requires value channel");
     }
     ```
     Note: `auto` changed to `AuxChannelPolicy` to make the short name `p` readable.

While efficient for the default kCapacity = 2, this loop could become a performance bottleneck if the capacity is increased for large types T. Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/ship-pr

# Ship PR

Create a feature branch from the current branch, commit all changes, push, and open a PR.

## Pre-flight

Verify we have changes to ship:

```bash
git status
git diff --stat
git diff --cached --stat
```

If there are no changes (staged or unstaged, tracked or untracked), stop and inform the user.

## Gather Context

```bash
git branch --show-current
git log --oneline -5
```

The current branch becomes the PR's **base branch** (the branch to merge into). If already on a feature branch, ask the user if they want to PR into the branch they branched from instead.

## Steps

### 1. Determine branch name and commit message

If the user provided arguments, use them to derive:
- **Branch name**: kebab-case, prefixed with `feat/`, `fix/`, or `refactor/` based on the change type. Max 50 chars.
- **Commit message**: conventional commit format from the same description.

If no arguments provided, analyze the diff to determine both:
- Read `git diff` and `git diff --cached` and any untracked files
- Classify the change (feat, fix, refactor, docs, test, chore)
- Draft a branch name and commit message

**Show the user the proposed branch name and commit message before proceeding.** Wait for approval.

### 2. Create branch and commit

```bash
git checkout -b <branch-name>
```

Stage all relevant files. **Do NOT stage:**
- `.env` files or credentials
- Large binary files unless they're clearly intentional (e.g., `.uf2` firmware)

```bash
git add <specific files>
git commit -m "<message>

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>"
```

Use a HEREDOC for multi-line commit messages.

### 3. Push

```bash
git push -u origin <branch-name>
```

### 4. Create PR

```bash
gh pr create --base <base-branch> --title "<title>" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
<1-3 bullet points describing the changes>

## Test plan
<How the changes were validated>


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
```

The PR body should summarize what changed and how it was tested. Reference any validation output if `/validate-smol` or similar was run.

### 5. Report

```
PR created: <url>
Branch: <branch-name> → <base-branch>
```

## Safety Rules

- **Never force push**
- **Never push to main/master directly** — always create a branch
- **Always show the branch name and commit message before creating them**
- **If the user is already on a feature branch with commits**, ask whether to create a new branch or use the current one
- Stage files explicitly — never use `git add -A` or `git add .`


ARGUMENTS: I want to have our plan reviewed by Gemini before we start the implementation

yes

```bash
~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/fetch_pr_comments.sh
```

Then process the output:

1. Parse the issue comments, review comments, and review summaries from the script output.
2. Itemize the list of comments in descending order, with the highest priority comments first.
3. For each comment, include its author and assess if it is a valid comment. If it is valid, provide a detailed description of how you would address the comment.

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history — you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture, design, and review tasks**: use the most capable available model.

**Task complexity signals:**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec → cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns → standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding → most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Proceed to spec compliance review.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Prompt Templates

- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra

[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ❌ Issues:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)

[Implementer fixes issues]
Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting

[Spec reviewer reviews again]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant now

[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Implementer fixes]
Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Code reviewer reviews again]
Code reviewer: ✅ Approved

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is ✅** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/Documents/Github/smol/.claude/skills/validate-smol

# Validate smol

Run all automated checks for the smol flight controller. Activate `~/.venvs/smol` before every command.

**CRITICAL**: Capture the FULL output of every command. Never re-run a command just to see which tests failed — read the output you already have. Use `2>&1` to merge stderr, and save long outputs to a file if needed.

## Checks

Run these in order. If a check fails, stop and report — do not continue to later checks.

### 1. Regenerate and format

Regenerate all generated files and run the formatter. This ensures we never commit stale generated code or unformatted source.

```bash
python3 protocol/generate.py && just format && just check-format && just check-codegen
```

Both checks must pass. Generated files (`src/protocol/`) are excluded from formatting — the generator owns their style.

### 2. Native unit tests

```bash
just test-native 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-native.txt
```

Read the FULL output to find failures. Do NOT re-run just to see which tests failed.

Report pass/fail count from the summary line (e.g., "118 test cases: 118 succeeded").

Known flaky: `test_smol_matches_full_after_full_sequence` (BUG-006, FullEkf nondeterminism). If ONLY this test fails, re-run once. If it fails twice, report it.

### 3. Thread sanitizer

```bash
just test-tsan 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-tsan.txt
```

Catches data races in SharedState, ring buffer, and cross-thread communication. Requires `setarch -R` for ASLR compatibility on Linux 6.x.

### 4. Elixir GCS tests

```bash
just test-gcs 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-gcs.txt
```

### 5. Native build

```bash
just build-native
```

### 6. RP2350B build (CMake)

```bash
just build-fc3
```

This catches missing source files in `CMakeLists.txt` and RP2350B-specific compilation errors that native builds miss.

### 7. Replay validation

```bash
just test-replay 2>&1 | tee /tmp/smol-test-replay.txt
```

This builds `native_full_ekf`, replays reference flight data through both EKFs, and checks RMS error bounds vs truth. Requires steps 4-5 to have passed.

## Report

```
## Validation Report

### Regenerate + format
[PASS — tree clean / PASS — N files updated / FAIL — details]

### Native tests
[PASS — N test cases: N succeeded / FAIL — details]

### Thread sanitizer
[PASS / FAIL — details]

### GCS tests
[PASS — N tests: N passed / FAIL — details]

### Native build
[PASS / FAIL]

### RP2350B build
[PASS / FAIL — details if failed]

### Replay validation
[PASS — N passed / FAIL — details]

### Summary
[READY TO SHIP / NEEDS FIXES — list what failed]
```

## Next Steps

- All pass: `/ship` to commit
- Failures: fix and re-run `/validate-smol`

   - Templated `GraphRuntime::Evaluate<Obs>` — shared between ObservationFeatures and future fast-loop SafetyObservation
