Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher

## Arguments (optional, free-form)

Parse these out of the user's invocation text:

- **`final`** — this is the final round of changes. Skip posting `/gemini review` AND skip launching the re-review watcher. Use when the user says things like "last round," "final," "no more reviews needed," "we're done," or explicitly passes `final` to the skill.
- **A PR number** (e.g. `87`) — fetch that specific PR rather than the one matching the current branch. The watcher callback hands this back when a new review arrives on a PR whose branch isn't currently checked out.
- **`--sonnet`** — opt out of the Opus default and run the review pass faster on Sonnet. Use this only when the workflow has been actively reliable (no API errors disrupting the watcher) and the comments look mechanical. Reasoning: Opus is the default because the watcher is meant to enable autonomous review loops; an API error that interrupts the watcher costs more time than Opus's slower output. Sonnet is fine for short, mechanical-decision rounds — but only if the parent session can actually dispatch a Sonnet variant. If you're not sure, leave the default.

Both are optional. Default: current-branch PR, non-final, Opus.

## Fetch pass

Run the helper script to fetch all PR comments since the latest commit:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/fetch_pr_comments.sh [PR_NUMBER]
```

Then process the output:

1. Parse the issue comments, review comments, review summaries, and review threads from the script output.
2. Itemize the list of comments in descending order, with the highest priority comments first.
3. For each comment, include its author and assess if it is a valid comment. If it is valid, provide a detailed description of how you would address the comment.

## After addressing the comments

Automated reviewers (Gemini Code Assist, Copilot, etc.) do not read the conversation and will keep re-surfacing the same concerns every review pass if the threads stay open. To prevent duplicate flags across rounds:

**After you have decided on every bot comment — whether applied or declined — close out the loop for each one.**

For each review comment from `gemini-code-assist[bot]` or `github-copilot` (or any bot author):

1. **Find the matching thread** in the `--- REVIEW THREADS ---` section of the script output by cross-referencing `comment_ids` with the comment `id` you processed. You need the `thread_id`.

2. **Post a short reply** to the thread explaining the decision. Use `gh api` against the PR's comments endpoint. For inline review comments, reply via REST:

   ```bash
   gh api -X POST \
     "repos/${OWNER}/${REPO}/pulls/${PR_NUMBER}/comments/${COMMENT_ID}/replies" \
     -f body="Applied in <SHORT_SHA>." \
     # or:
     -f body="Declined — <one-line rationale, e.g. 'fallback pattern, against project CLAUDE.md'>."
   ```

3. **Resolve the thread** via GraphQL (REST can't do this):

   ```bash
   gh api graphql -f query='
   mutation($id: ID!) {
     resolveReviewThread(input: { threadId: $id }) {
       thread { id isResolved }
     }
   }' -F id="${THREAD_ID}"
   ```

Do this for **every bot comment you addressed**, even the ones you declined. Leaving a declined-with-rationale reply on a resolved thread is what stops the same comment from coming back next round.

Human comments should NOT be auto-resolved — leave those for the human reviewer to resolve after they've seen your response.

## After pushing fixes: re-invoke Gemini

If you pushed new commits to address review comments, post a `/gemini review` comment on the PR so Gemini Code Assist re-runs against the latest commits. Without this, the bot only reviews the original push and your fixes stay unverified.

```bash
gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "/gemini review"
```

Do this **once per PR, after all fixes are pushed**. Not per-thread — one comment triggers a full re-review.

Skip this step if any of these hold:
- You pushed no new commits (e.g., all bot comments were declined with replies, no code changed).
- User passed `final` — this is the last round, no more reviews needed.

     ```cpp
     inline constexpr uint32_t kSlowLoopPeriodMs = 50;
     struct SlowLoopInputs {
       float north_m, east_m, down_m, vn_mps, ve_mps, airspeed_mps;
       SensorHealth sensors;
       FlightMode mode;
       uint32_t current_elapsed_ms;

     };
     class SlowLoop {
      public:
       void Init(WorldProgress* world, MissionPlanner* planner);
       void Step();
       void Run();
       void Stop();
       bool IsRunning() const;
      private:
       SlowLoopInputs BuildInputs(const WorldProgress& world);
       void Tick(const SlowLoopInputs& inputs, WorldProgress* world);
       WorldProgress* _world = nullptr;
       MissionPlanner* _mission_planner = nullptr;
       FastLoopMiniSnapshot _latest_snapshot{};
       bool _have_snapshot = false;
       std::atomic<bool> _stop{false};
       std::atomic<bool> _running{false};
     };
     ```

   ```bash
     -f body="Applied in <SHORT_SHA>." \
     # or:
     -f body="Declined — <one-line rationale, e.g. 'fallback pattern, against project CLAUDE.md'>."
   ```

3. **Resolve the thread** via GraphQL (REST can't do this):

   ```bash
   gh api graphql -f query='
   mutation($id: ID!) {
     resolveReviewThread(input: { threadId: $id }) {
       thread { id isResolved }
     }
   }' -F id="${THREAD_ID}"
   ```

Do this for **every bot comment you addressed**, even the ones you declined. Leaving a declined-with-rationale reply on a resolved thread is what stops the same comment from coming back next round.

Human comments should NOT be auto-resolved — leave those for the human reviewer to resolve after they've seen your response.

## After pushing fixes: re-invoke Gemini

If you pushed new commits to address review comments, post a `/gemini review` comment on the PR so Gemini Code Assist re-runs against the latest commits. Without this, the bot only reviews the original push and your fixes stay unverified.

```bash
gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "/gemini review"
```

Do this **once per PR, after all fixes are pushed**. Not per-thread — one comment triggers a full re-review.

Skip this step if any of these hold:
- You pushed no new commits (e.g., all bot comments were declined with replies, no code changed).
- User passed `final` — this is the last round, no more reviews needed.

## Auto-resume on next Gemini review

When you expect another Gemini review (commits pushed + `/gemini review` posted + not `final`), launch a background watcher per PR and let me auto-resume when it fires. This closes the loop without the user having to tell me "Gemini just posted."

1. **Launch the watcher in the background** for each PR touched:

   ```bash
   ~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/watch_pr_reviews.sh ${PR_NUMBER}
   ```

   Invoke via `Bash(run_in_background=true)`. Capture the returned `shell_id`. The script polls every 30 s, exits 0 on the first new bot review, times out after 2 hours.

2. **Stream it with `Monitor`** on the returned shell_id. Each line of stdout is a notification you'll receive:
   - `WATCHING pr=N baseline=...` — initial setup line, ignore.
   - `NEW_REVIEW pr=N submitted_at=... state=...` — fire signal: a fresh Gemini review has landed.
   - `TIMEOUT pr=N ...` (stderr) — 2-hour cap hit; tell the user and stop.

3. **When a `NEW_REVIEW` arrives**, auto-re-invoke this skill scoped to that PR:

   ```
   Skill(cp-pr-fetcher, args="${PR_NUMBER}")
   ```

   The skill handles its own fetch → address → push → re-trigger → watch cycle from there. Multiple PRs touched in one pass → one watcher per PR, process in whatever order they fire.

Skip the watcher if:
- You skipped the `/gemini review` step above for any reason (no commits, or `final`).
- The user explicitly told you not to wait for another review.

## Guidance

- Keep replies terse. "Applied in abc1234." or "Declined — useMemo is redundant under React Compiler." is enough.
- If you applied a fix, reference the commit SHA so the thread records the connection.
- If you declined, give the one-line reason that cites the project convention or doc (e.g. "CLAUDE.md: no fallbacks", "spec: reducer is the validation point").
- Batch the reply + resolve after you have finished the itemized analysis. Do not intersperse — finish the review pass, then close the loop in one sweep.


ARGUMENTS: 95

<output-file>REDACTED.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Background command "Watch PR 95 for round-2 review" completed (exit code 0)</summary>
</task-notification>

---

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher

## Arguments (optional, free-form)

Parse these out of the user's invocation text:

- **`final`** — this is the final round of changes. Skip posting `/gemini review` AND skip launching the re-review watcher. Use when the user says things like "last round," "final," "no more reviews needed," "we're done," or explicitly passes `final` to the skill.
- **A PR number** (e.g. `87`) — fetch that specific PR rather than the one matching the current branch. The watcher callback hands this back when a new review arrives on a PR whose branch isn't currently checked out.
- **`--sonnet`** — opt out of the Opus default and run the review pass faster on Sonnet. Use this only when the workflow has been actively reliable (no API errors disrupting the watcher) and the comments look mechanical. Reasoning: Opus is the default because the watcher is meant to enable autonomous review loops; an API error that interrupts the watcher costs more time than Opus's slower output. Sonnet is fine for short, mechanical-decision rounds — but only if the parent session can actually dispatch a Sonnet variant. If you're not sure, leave the default.

Both are optional. Default: current-branch PR, non-final, Opus.

## Fetch pass

Run the helper script to fetch all PR comments since the latest commit:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/fetch_pr_comments.sh [PR_NUMBER]
```

Then process the output:

1. Parse the issue comments, review comments, review summaries, and review threads from the script output.
2. Itemize the list of comments in descending order, with the highest priority comments first.
3. For each comment, include its author and assess if it is a valid comment. If it is valid, provide a detailed description of how you would address the comment.

## After addressing the comments

Automated reviewers (Gemini Code Assist, Copilot, etc.) do not read the conversation and will keep re-surfacing the same concerns every review pass if the threads stay open. To prevent duplicate flags across rounds:

**After you have decided on every bot comment — whether applied or declined — close out the loop for each one.**

For each review comment from `gemini-code-assist[bot]` or `github-copilot` (or any bot author):

1. **Find the matching thread** in the `--- REVIEW THREADS ---` section of the script output by cross-referencing `comment_ids` with the comment `id` you processed. You need the `thread_id`.

2. **Post a short reply** to the thread explaining the decision. Use `gh api` against the PR's comments endpoint. For inline review comments, reply via REST:

   ```bash
   gh api -X POST \
     "repos/${OWNER}/${REPO}/pulls/${PR_NUMBER}/comments/${COMMENT_ID}/replies" \
     -f body="Applied in <SHORT_SHA>." \
     # or:
     -f body="Declined — <one-line rationale, e.g. 'fallback pattern, against project CLAUDE.md'>."
   ```

3. **Resolve the thread** via GraphQL (REST can't do this):

   ```bash
   gh api graphql -f query='
   mutation($id: ID!) {
     resolveReviewThread(input: { threadId: $id }) {
       thread { id isResolved }
     }
   }' -F id="${THREAD_ID}"
   ```

Do this for **every bot comment you addressed**, even the ones you declined. Leaving a declined-with-rationale reply on a resolved thread is what stops the same comment from coming back next round.

Human comments should NOT be auto-resolved — leave those for the human reviewer to resolve after they've seen your response.

## After pushing fixes: re-invoke Gemini

If you pushed new commits to address review comments, post a `/gemini review` comment on the PR so Gemini Code Assist re-runs against the latest commits. Without this, the bot only reviews the original push and your fixes stay unverified.

```bash
gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "/gemini review"
```

Do this **once per PR, after all fixes are pushed**. Not per-thread — one comment triggers a full re-review.

Skip this step if any of these hold:
- You pushed no new commits (e.g., all bot comments were declined with replies, no code changed).
- User passed `final` — this is the last round, no more reviews needed.

## Auto-resume on next Gemini review

ARGUMENTS: 95

---

The code from Tick should be put inside Step. Right now, Step just calls Tick, which is an extra layer of obfuscation:
void SlowLoop::Step() {
  Tick(BuildInputs(*_world), _world);
}

void SlowLoop::Run() {
  uint64_t deadline_us = hal::clock::MonotonicNowUs();
  const uint64_t period_us = static_cast<uint64_t>(kSlowLoopPeriodMs) * 1000;
  while (!_stop.load(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
    Step();
    deadline_us += period_us;
    hal::clock::SleepUntil(deadline_us);
  }
  _running.store(false, std::memory_order_release);
}

SlowLoopInputs SlowLoop::BuildInputs(const WorldProgress& world) {

1. **Launch the watcher in the background** for each PR touched:

   ```bash
   ~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/watch_pr_reviews.sh ${PR_NUMBER}
   ```

   Invoke via `Bash(run_in_background=true)`. Capture the returned `shell_id`. The script polls every 30 s, exits 0 on the first new bot review, times out after 2 hours.

2. **Stream it with `Monitor`** on the returned shell_id. Each line of stdout is a notification you'll receive:
   - `WATCHING pr=N baseline=...` — initial setup line, ignore.
   - `NEW_REVIEW pr=N submitted_at=... state=...` — fire signal: a fresh Gemini review has landed.
   - `TIMEOUT pr=N ...` (stderr) — 2-hour cap hit; tell the user and stop.

3. **When a `NEW_REVIEW` arrives**, auto-re-invoke this skill scoped to that PR:

   ```
   Skill(cp-pr-fetcher, args="${PR_NUMBER}")
   ```

   The skill handles its own fetch → address → push → re-trigger → watch cycle from there. Multiple PRs touched in one pass → one watcher per PR, process in whatever order they fire.

Skip the watcher if:
- You skipped the `/gemini review` step above for any reason (no commits, or `final`).
- The user explicitly told you not to wait for another review.

## Guidance

- Keep replies terse. "Applied in abc1234." or "Declined — useMemo is redundant under React Compiler." is enough.
- If you applied a fix, reference the commit SHA so the thread records the connection.
- If you declined, give the one-line reason that cites the project convention or doc (e.g. "CLAUDE.md: no fallbacks", "spec: reducer is the validation point").
- Batch the reply + resolve after you have finished the itemized analysis. Do not intersperse — finish the review pass, then close the loop in one sweep.

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-plan

# cp-plan

Multi-phase planning skill that turns a design spec into a sequence of small, independently reviewable PRs — and proves the decomposition works by dry-running the test phase against the planned types before any code ships.

## When to use

- A brainstorming design spec exists at `docs/superpowers/specs/...-design.md`.
- The work would otherwise become one large feature branch (>10 files, multiple distinct concerns).
- You want review checkpoints between chunks of work, not just at the end.

Do NOT use cp-plan for:

- Pure bug fixes touching a single file or a single mechanical refactor sweep — go straight to `superpowers:writing-plans`.
- Greenfield work without a brainstorming spec — run `/superpowers:brainstorming` first.
- Per-PR step-by-step planning — that's `superpowers:writing-plans`'s job, invoked from `--refine`.
- **Small features.** If the total scope is ≤10 files across ≤2 distinct concerns, cp-plan's multi-phase ceremony has more overhead than benefit — go straight to `superpowers:writing-plans`. Heuristic: if you can't think of three distinct PRs that each have a different reviewer-question, the work is too small.

### Special case: non-executable deliverables

For markdown skills, configuration, prompts, or any deliverable without a unit-test framework (GTest, pytest, ExUnit), the **test phase is degenerate** — there is no `DISABLED_` marker mechanism to apply. In these cases:

- Replace the test-phase pseudocode section with a **behavioral spec** describing expected behavior in plain prose: "given X, when invoked with Y, the deliverable produces Z."
- The dogfood run *is* the test. Capture observed-vs-predicted behavior in the refinement log.
- The size threshold above usually rules out cp-plan for these deliverables anyway. If you find yourself running cp-plan on a markdown-only spec, double-check whether `superpowers:writing-plans` would serve better.

## Modes

### Initial mode: `/cp-plan <spec-path>`

Decompose a fresh design spec into a master plan. Output: one markdown file at `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature>-cp-plan.md` based on the template at `~/.claude/skills/cp-plan/templates/cp-plan-template.md`.

### Refinement mode: `/cp-plan --refine <PR#|slug>`

Once a planned PR's dependencies have merged, refine that single PR into an executable plan via `superpowers:writing-plans`. Append a refinement-log entry to the master plan file.

### Status mode: `/cp-plan --status`

Read the master plan file. For each PR in the sequence table, query git log for a commit subject matching the title slug. Update the `Status` column to `merged` for matches.

## Initial mode flow

1. **Read the design spec** in full. Extract: feature name, the units of work it implies (modules, classes, integrations), and any explicit non-goals.

2. **Read the template** at `~/.claude/skills/cp-plan/templates/cp-plan-template.md`. Use as the structural skeleton.

3. **Decompose into PRs** following this canonical order:
   - **PR 1: Types and interfaces.** All new structs, classes (declarations only), enums, function signatures, build wiring for new compilation units. No bodies. No tests. Builds green.
   - **PR 2: Integration wiring.** Plumb the new types through existing modules — slow-loop registration, queue declarations, builder injection, parameter store entries, etc. No business logic. Builds green.
   - **PR 3: Test contract.** Test cases asserting expected behavior, marked `DISABLED_` (GTest), `@pytest.mark.skip(reason="awaiting <PR# or feature>")` (pytest), or `@tag :pending` (ExUnit). Tests are real code that compiles, but the markers keep CI green.
   - **PR 4..N: Implementation chunks.** Each chunk turns a coherent subset of the disabled tests green. Target ≤10 files per chunk; explicitly label refactor-sweep PRs with one of: `rename`, `move`, `type-rename`, `signature-prop`, `format-only`.

   Splitting heuristics:
   - If two tests would share the same impl change, they belong in one chunk.
   - If a chunk would touch >10 non-refactor files, split by responsibility (not by line-count).
   - If a single PR is doing two distinct things, split.

4. **Plan PR 1 at executable detail** in the master plan file: every type, every signature, every file path, every build wiring change.

5. **Plan PRs 2..N as summaries only.** Title, file list (target ≤10), 2-4 sentence "what" description. No executable detail. The user (or future cp-plan invocations) will run `/cp-plan --refine <PR#>` after dependency PRs merge to spawn writing-plans-format executable plans.

6. **Write the test-phase dry-run** — pseudocode test cases for the test-contract PR, using PR 1 types verbatim. Include the `DISABLED_` (or skip / pending) markers in the pseudocode so it copy-pastes into the real test files. Aim for completeness: every behavior the impl chunks must satisfy lives in this section.

7. **Write the impl-phase dry-run** — narrative prose per impl PR. Which disabled tests it enables, files it touches, why this chunk.

8. **Run the dry-run self-check.** Answer the three questions inline:
   - Can every test be written using only PR 1 types?
   - Can every impl chunk be done in ≤10 files (except labeled refactor sweeps)?
   - Is any PR doing two things?

   If any answer is "no," **iterate** — fix the earlier section, re-answer. Do not write the file with "no" answers; the dry-run is the gate, not a checkbox.

9. **Write the master plan file** to `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature>-cp-plan.md`.

10. **Commit the master plan file** on the current branch with subject `docs(plan): cp-plan for <feature>`. Do NOT push (per project rule).

## Refinement mode flow (`--refine <PR#|slug>`)

1. Locate the master plan file (most recent `*-cp-plan.md` in `docs/superpowers/plans/` unless ambiguous — if ambiguous, ask).
2. Find the named PR in the sequence table. Verify all rows in the `Depends on` column for this PR have `Status = merged`. If not, refuse and report which dependency is unmerged.
3. Invoke `superpowers:writing-plans` with two inputs: the master plan file and the specific PR's row + summary block. The output goes to `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature>-pr<N>-<slug>-plan.md`.
4. Append a refinement log entry to the master plan file:

```markdown
### {{YYYY-MM-DD}} — PR {{N}} refined
- Spawned plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/.../<spawned>.md`
- Drift from dry-run: {{describe any reality-vs-prediction differences, or "none"}}
```

   The "drift" entry is required even if "none" — empty drift entries are signal that the dry-run is predictive.

5. Update the row's `Status` to `refined`.
6. Commit the master plan update with subject `docs(plan): refine PR <N> (<slug>)`.

## Status mode flow (`--status`)

1. Read the master plan file.
2. For each PR row with `Status = planned` or `refined`, run:
   ```bash
   git log --oneline main..HEAD --grep="<slug>"
   ```
   If a commit subject contains the slug, set `Status = merged`.
3. If any row's status changed, write the updated file. If nothing changed, report "no changes."

This mode uses commit-subject heuristics. Slugs may collide; if a row's "merged" detection looks wrong, report it and ask.

## Output discipline

- **Use feature-meaningful PR titles.** "MissionPlanner types and PlannedLeg struct," not "Phase 1." The internal phase concept stays internal.
- **The dry-run pseudocode is real code in pseudocode form.** Use real types from PR 1, real method signatures, real assertion macros. The test PR will copy-paste this with minor edits.
- **The refinement log is append-only.** Past entries are retrospective material; never delete.

## What cp-plan does not do

- Does not run brainstorming. The spec is an input.
- Does not run any executable detail planning itself — `--refine` invokes `superpowers:writing-plans`.
- Does not implement code. cp-plan emits plan artifacts only.
- Does not push commits. Per project rule, all commits stay local until the user requests `git push`.

## Required reading before invoking

- The design spec at the path passed in.
- `~/.claude/skills/cp-plan/templates/cp-plan-template.md`.
- `~/.claude/skills/code-writing-guidelines/SKILL.md` (for the "Plan in three layers" + "Validate by dry-running tests" sections).
- The smol repo's CLAUDE.md (for project-specific naming and style conventions referenced in the plan).


ARGUMENTS: --refine 5

---

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans — one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## No Placeholders

Every step must contain the actual content an engineer needs. These are **plan failures** — never write them:
- "TBD", "TODO", "implement later", "fill in details"
- "Add appropriate error handling" / "add validation" / "handle edge cases"
- "Write tests for the above" (without actual test code)
- "Similar to Task N" (repeat the code — the engineer may be reading tasks out of order)
- Steps that describe what to do without showing how (code blocks required for code steps)
- References to types, functions, or methods not defined in any task

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in every step — if a step changes code, show the code
- Exact commands with expected output
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Self-Review

After writing the complete plan, look at the spec with fresh eyes and check the plan against it. This is a checklist you run yourself — not a subagent dispatch.

**1. Spec coverage:** Skim each section/requirement in the spec. Can you point to a task that implements it? List any gaps.

**2. Placeholder scan:** Search your plan for red flags — any of the patterns from the "No Placeholders" section above. Fix them.

**3. Type consistency:** Do the types, method signatures, and property names you used in later tasks match what you defined in earlier tasks? A function called `clearLayers()` in Task 3 but `clearFullLayers()` in Task 7 is a bug.

If you find issues, fix them inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on. If you find a spec requirement with no task, add the task.

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review

DRIFT FROM MASTER PLAN (writing-plans pass MUST capture all of this):

1. **`fly_to_waypoint` and `take_off` skill files DO NOT EXIST on post-PR-4 main.** Only `idle` and `loiter_at_point` are in `src/mission/skills/`. The master plan's PR 5 file list includes `test/test_fly_to_waypoint/test_fly_to_waypoint.cpp` (modify) and `test/test_phase3_skill_sequence/test_phase3_skill_sequence.cpp` (modify) and `test/test_phase3_racetrack/test_phase3_racetrack.cpp` (modify), plus implicitly `test_take_off`. None of these test directories exist; the underlying skills they would test don't exist either. Trying to write tests that reference `fly_to_waypoint::Run` or `take_off::Run` won't compile.

   **Resolution:** PR 5 ships only the test scaffolding for things that CAN compile today:
   - (A) `test/test_mission_planner_climbout/test_mission_planner_climbout.cpp` (NEW) — full DISABLED_ test set per the master plan's pseudocode, adapted for actual API.

   The master plan's PRs 6/7/8 (impl chunks) will each carry their own DISABLED_ test additions alongside the skill files they create. Document this scope split in the plan's drift section AND suggest the cp-plan refinement-log entry capture it.

2. **`MissionPlanner` has no public `SetOrigin` method.** Origin arrives via the `etl::message_router` route — public surface is `on_receive(const OriginUpdatedMsg&)`. The master plan's pseudocode at line 230 uses `planner.SetOrigin(MakeTestOrigin())` which doesn't exist. Tests need to either:
   - Dispatch `OriginUpdatedMsg` through a `Bus` instance to the planner, OR
   - Add a test-only helper / friend declaration / public seam.

   Verify on `src/navigation/mission_planner.h` (current main HEAD) what's actually available. Existing `test_mission_controller` tests already construct a `Bus` and route `OriginUpdatedMsg` — copy that pattern.

3. **`SkillContext::current_planned_leg` is `const PlannedLeg*`** (PR 4 round 3 changed this). Master plan's pseudocode at lines 313-336 uses `ctx.current_planned_leg = MakePlannedLegStub(...)` (assigns by value) — this won't compile. Tests must assign pointer: `PlannedLeg leg = ...; ctx.current_planned_leg = &leg;`. Also the assertion `EXPECT_FALSE(cmd.primary.has_value())` is fine because `cmd.primary` is `std::optional<PlannedLeg>` (the OWNING storage on `SkillCommand`, distinct from the non-owning view on `SkillContext`).

   **Caveat:** the FlyToWaypoint test cases (lines 313-336) belong to a future PR anyway since `fly_to_waypoint` skill doesn't exist. Note this in the drift entry for completeness, but don't include those tests in PR 5.

4. **`SlowLoopInputs::current_planned_leg` is `std::optional<PlannedLeg>`** (the owning storage). The Phase3Sequence test pseudocode at lines 341-351 references `SlowLoopInputBuilder` — but `SlowLoopInputBuilder` class no longer exists (PR #95 consolidation collapsed it into `SlowLoop`). Tests would now use `SlowLoop slow_loop; slow_loop.Init(&world, &planner); slow_loop.Step();`. But again, this test belongs to a future PR (Phase3Sequence depends on multiple skills).

5. **`MissionPlanner::SetClimbout` takes `const std::optional<dubins::Waypoint>&`** (PR 4 round 2 change). Master plan's pseudocode at line 244 passes a bare `Waypoint`; implicit conversion to `std::optional<Waypoint>` still works, so tests compile, but the helper `MakeClimboutNorthOfOrigin()` should return either a `Waypoint` or `std::optional<Waypoint>` consistently.

6. **`PlannedLeg.leg_index` field — verify shape**. The master plan's pseudocode asserts `leg->leg_index == 0` (line 255) and `leg->leg_index == 1` (line 268). Verify `PlannedLeg` actually has a `leg_index` field on post-PR-4 main; if it does, the tests are fine as-is. If it doesn't (e.g., the field is `index` or named differently), the plan must adjust.

7. **`PlannedLeg::leg` field is `dubins::MissionLeg`** — for asserting "Start of leg ≈ climbout NED position" (pseudocode line 257), figure out the actual field path. Don't leave the comment "Start of leg ≈ climbout NED position" as a placeholder; either pick a concrete assertion (e.g., the first segment's start point matches climbout) or document why it's loose. The cp-plan emphasizes "complete code in every step" — comment-only assertions are placeholders and a plan failure.

8. **`MissionStore` shape and helper `MakeThreeWaypointMission()`** — verify what fixture-builder pattern existing tests use. Look at `test_mission_planner` (does not exist on main per the find above), `test_mission_controller`, `test_mission_store`. Pick the pattern that matches the rest of the test corpus.

9. **`NedOrigin` shape and helper `MakeTestOrigin()`** — verify how existing tests construct an origin. There's a `NedOrigin::FromGps` helper — match the existing convention.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - cp-plan multi-PR decomposition (types → wiring → tests → impl chunks)
   - cp-pr-fetcher skill auto-watcher loop (Opus default, --sonnet opt-out)
   - `etl::queue_spsc_atomic` push semantics, drain-to-latest consumer-side contract
   - ADR-A (drain-to-latest), ADR-F (slow-loop staleness/mode-agnostic liveness)
   - `feedback_value_or_copy_trap` — named local if/else binds zero-copy; value_or and ternary T{} both copy
   - Squash-merge with `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch`
   - `git reset --soft HEAD~1` to remove unpushed commit from history
   - ADR conventions: short memo (400-800 words), single-letter IDs A-G (next H), `Decision status:` header
   - HYP-1 (FC pilot-flyable without GCS), HYP-9 (cross-language byte-identical), HYP-10 (Rust portability discipline), HYP-11 (own the vertical)
   - shared-planning ADR-H: single C++ planning library + Erlang Port + TOML-codegen'd binary frames sidecar
   - Integer-quantized hash basis preserves HYP-9 across cross-target divergence
   - 3-hash version-skew check (wire_protocol_hash + planning_library_hash + MAVLink protocol_hash)
   - MissionPlanner uses `etl::message_router` pattern (OriginUpdatedMsg, CompletedLegMsg, CaptureWaypointMsg)
   - SkillContext::current_planned_leg is `const PlannedLeg*` (non-owning view); SlowLoopInputs::current_planned_leg is `std::optional<PlannedLeg>` (owning storage)
   - SlowLoopInputBuilder was deleted in PR #95 consolidation; SlowLoop class now owns build+tick

     ```cpp
     std::optional<PlannedLeg> MissionPlanner::PlanLeg(const int leg_index) const {
       if (!_mission.HasMission() || !_origin.has_value()) return std::nullopt;
       if (leg_index < 0 || leg_index >= _mission.waypoint_count) return std::nullopt;
       if (leg_index == 0 && !_climbout.has_value()) return std::nullopt;
       const dubins::Waypoint start = leg_index == 0 ? *_climbout : ConvertToNed(_mission.waypoints[leg_index - 1]);
       const dubins::Waypoint end = ConvertToNed(_mission.waypoints[leg_index]);
       PlannedLeg planned = {};
       planned.leg = PlanFromNedWaypoints(start, end);
       planned.leg_index = leg_index;
       planned.total_legs = _mission.waypoint_count;
       planned.estimated_time_s = planned.leg.speed_mps > 0.0f ? planned.leg.path.total_length_m / planned.leg.speed_mps : 0.0f;
       return planned;
     }
     ```
   - Dropped gate's second condition: `if (_next_leg_index >= total_legs) return std::nullopt;`

   ```cpp
   #include "mission/skills/take_off.h"
   namespace take_off {
   void Run(const SkillContext& ctx, WorldProgress* world, SkillCommand* cmd) {
     TakeoffState& takeoff = world->phase.takeoff;
     if (takeoff.phase == TakeoffPhase::kIdle && ctx.launch_requested) {
       takeoff.phase = TakeoffPhase::kArmed;
       // Climbout-write lands in PR 7b — needs psi_rad on the Mini.
     }
     cmd->safety.armed = takeoff.phase != TakeoffPhase::kIdle;
     cmd->safety.takeoff_phase = static_cast<uint8_t>(takeoff.phase);
   }
   }  // namespace take_off
   ```

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher

## Arguments (optional, free-form)

Parse these out of the user's invocation text:

- **`final`** — this is the final round of changes. Skip posting `/gemini review` AND skip launching the re-review watcher. Use when the user says things like "last round," "final," "no more reviews needed," "we're done," or explicitly passes `final` to the skill.
- **A PR number** (e.g. `87`) — fetch that specific PR rather than the one matching the current branch. The watcher callback hands this back when a new review arrives on a PR whose branch isn't currently checked out.
- **`--sonnet`** — opt out of the Opus default and run the review pass faster on Sonnet. Use this only when the workflow has been actively reliable (no API errors disrupting the watcher) and the comments look mechanical. Reasoning: Opus is the default because the watcher is meant to enable autonomous review loops; an API error that interrupts the watcher costs more time than Opus's slower output. Sonnet is fine for short, mechanical-decision rounds — but only if the parent session can actually dispatch a Sonnet variant. If you're not sure, leave the default.

Both are optional. Default: current-branch PR, non-final, Opus.

## Fetch pass

Run the helper script to fetch all PR comments since the latest commit:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/fetch_pr_comments.sh [PR_NUMBER]
```

Then process the output:

1. Parse the issue comments, review comments, review summaries, and review threads from the script output.
2. Itemize the list of comments in descending order, with the highest priority comments first.
3. For each comment, include its author and assess if it is a valid comment. If it is valid, provide a detailed description of how you would address the comment.

## After addressing the comments

Automated reviewers (Gemini Code Assist, Copilot, etc.) do not read the conversation and will keep re-surfacing the same concerns every review pass if the threads stay open. To prevent duplicate flags across rounds:

**After you have decided on every bot comment — whether applied or declined — close out the loop for each one.**

For each review comment from `gemini-code-assist[bot]` or `github-copilot` (or any bot author):

1. **Find the matching thread** in the `--- REVIEW THREADS ---` section of the script output by cross-referencing `comment_ids` with the comment `id` you processed. You need the `thread_id`.

2. **Post a short reply** to the thread explaining the decision. Use `gh api` against the PR's comments endpoint. For inline review comments, reply via REST:

   ```bash
   gh api -X POST \
     "repos/${OWNER}/${REPO}/pulls/${PR_NUMBER}/comments/${COMMENT_ID}/replies" \
     -f body="Applied in <SHORT_SHA>." \
     # or:
     -f body="Declined — <one-line rationale, e.g. 'fallback pattern, against project CLAUDE.md'>."
   ```

3. **Resolve the thread** via GraphQL (REST can't do this):

   ```bash
   gh api graphql -f query='
   mutation($id: ID!) {
     resolveReviewThread(input: { threadId: $id }) {
       thread { id isResolved }
     }
   }' -F id="${THREAD_ID}"
   ```

Do this for **every bot comment you addressed**, even the ones you declined. Leaving a declined-with-rationale reply on a resolved thread is what stops the same comment from coming back next round.

Human comments should NOT be auto-resolved — leave those for the human reviewer to resolve after they've seen your response.

## After pushing fixes: re-invoke Gemini

If you pushed new commits to address review comments, post a `/gemini review` comment on the PR so Gemini Code Assist re-runs against the latest commits. Without this, the bot only reviews the original push and your fixes stay unverified.

```bash
gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "/gemini review"
```

Do this **once per PR, after all fixes are pushed**. Not per-thread — one comment triggers a full re-review.

Skip this step if any of these hold:
- You pushed no new commits (e.g., all bot comments were declined with replies, no code changed).
- User passed `final` — this is the last round, no more reviews needed.

## Auto-resume on next Gemini review

When you expect another Gemini review (commits pushed + `/gemini review` posted + not `final`), launch a background watcher per PR and let me auto-resume when it fires. This closes the loop without the user having to tell me "Gemini just posted."

1. **Launch the watcher in the background** for each PR touched:

   ```bash
   ~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/watch_pr_reviews.sh ${PR_NUMBER}
   ```

   Invoke via `Bash(run_in_background=true)`. Capture the returned `shell_id`. The script polls every 30 s, exits 0 on the first new bot review, times out after 2 hours.

2. **Stream it with `Monitor`** on the returned shell_id. Each line of stdout is a notification you'll receive:
   - `WATCHING pr=N baseline=...` — initial setup line, ignore.
   - `NEW_REVIEW pr=N submitted_at=... state=...` — fire signal: a fresh Gemini review has landed.
   - `TIMEOUT pr=N ...` (stderr) — 2-hour cap hit; tell the user and stop.

3. **When a `NEW_REVIEW` arrives**, auto-re-invoke this skill scoped to that PR:

   ```
   Skill(cp-pr-fetcher, args="${PR_NUMBER}")
   ```

   The skill handles its own fetch → address → push → re-trigger → watch cycle from there. Multiple PRs touched in one pass → one watcher per PR, process in whatever order they fire.

Skip the watcher if:
- You skipped the `/gemini review` step above for any reason (no commits, or `final`).
- The user explicitly told you not to wait for another review.

## Guidance

- Keep replies terse. "Applied in abc1234." or "Declined — useMemo is redundant under React Compiler." is enough.
- If you applied a fix, reference the commit SHA so the thread records the connection.
- If you declined, give the one-line reason that cites the project convention or doc (e.g. "CLAUDE.md: no fallbacks", "spec: reducer is the validation point").
- Batch the reply + resolve after you have finished the itemized analysis. Do not intersperse — finish the review pass, then close the loop in one sweep.

This altitude bug has been lurking. We have been using all over the place without explicity stating its reference point. For the sake of brevity, our convention should be that altitude_m refers to MSL, and agl_m refers to AGL. We already have an ENH in the backlog for determining AGL based on terrain data. That change should build a model for determining the ground level MSL for a given lat/lon. Until then, we rely on our NED Origin altitude as the reference altitude, and all AGLs must be based on that origin. please repeat to me your understanding of these concepts

---

Let's use just "_agl_m" instead of "_altitude_agl_m".
We also should have the AGL/MSL logic contained in a single place. We need to always have an estimate of the MSL of the ground beneath the aircraft. For example, if we have a downward facing lidar sensor, that will give as a way to estimate the ground MSL. Simiarly, terrain data will help if the lidar isn't available. But the ground MSL estimate should be constantly updated, likely using a kalman filter. That is beyond the scope of this change, but we should consider adding the scaffolding, so we can at least retrieve the AGL for a given lat/lon/altitude_m.

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-review

# Code Review

Review all changes on the current branch against main.

## Process

### 1. Gather Changes

```bash
git diff main --name-only
```

If no changes, inform the user and stop.

For each changed file, read the FULL file (not just the diff) to understand context. Also run:

```bash
git diff main
```

### 2. Review Against Guidelines

#### Code Reads Like Prose
- Descriptive function and variable names — no abbreviations that require mental decoding
- Linear data flows — can you read top-to-bottom without jumping around?
- Efficient line count — no bloat, no unnecessary abstractions
- DRY — is anything duplicated that shouldn't be?
- Single responsibility — each function does ONE thing, each class has ONE purpose
- Testable — could you write a test for this without mocking half the system?

#### Make Invalid States Unrepresentable
- Constraints encoded in types, not runtime checks
- No scattered validation for the same invariant
- `std::optional`, enums, and constructors used to enforce validity

#### Trace the Data
- Where does each value come from? What constraints does it carry?
- Are there redundant conversions or unnecessary intermediates?
- Does every downstream consumer get what it expects?

#### Always Make It Better
- Did we touch code without improving it?
- Are there obvious improvements just outside the diff boundary? (note in suggestions, don't block)

#### Project Conventions (from CLAUDE.md)
- No docstrings unless asked
- No fallbacks — code works or fails loudly
- No dead code, no commented-out experiments
- No default arguments
- Leading underscores for class data members
- PascalCase functions, snake_case locals, kCamelCase constants
- "latest" not "last" for most recent values
- Parameter passing: pointer for mutation, const ref for read-only, const value for scalars

### 3. Check For Slop
- Comments that explain "what" instead of "why"
- Defensive checks for conditions that can't happen
- Unnecessary try/catch or error handling beyond system boundaries
- Verbose logging that doesn't match existing patterns
- Style inconsistencies with surrounding code

### 4. Multi-phase Planning Discipline (cp-plan-aware)

If a master cp-plan file exists for this feature (look for `docs/superpowers/plans/*-cp-plan.md`), apply these checks. If no cp-plan file exists, skip this section and proceed.

#### File count vs. plan target
- Count files changed: `git diff main --name-only | wc -l`.
- Find the row in the cp-plan PR sequence table matching this PR's title or branch slug.
- Compare actual count to the plan's target.
- If actual > plan + 2 AND `Refactor sweep? = no` for this row: **flag as planning failure.** The cp-plan dry-run self-check missed this. Recommend splitting the PR or labeling it as a refactor sweep with a category.
- Refactor-sweep PRs are exempt from the size cap if the row is labeled with one of: `rename`, `move`, `type-rename`, `signature-prop`, `format-only`.

#### DISABLED_ marker discipline
- For impl-phase PRs (chunks 4..N in the cp-plan), count `DISABLED_` removals: `git diff main -- '*test*' | grep -c '^-.*DISABLED_'`.
- Count new tests added: `git diff main -- '*test*' | grep -c '^+TEST('`.
- If new tests added > markers removed, flag as a heuristic warning: impl PRs should primarily activate existing contracts, not add new ones. Acceptable exceptions: regression tests for bugs found during impl, integration tests not in the contract.
- Test-contract PRs (the "Test contract" row) are exempt — they ADD `DISABLED_` markers as their primary purpose.

#### Refinement log entry exists for refined PRs
- If this PR's row has `Status = refined`, verify the master cp-plan file has a refinement log entry pointing to a spawned writing-plans doc.
- If `Status = refined` but no log entry exists, flag as a process violation.
- Drift notes in the log entry can be "none" — but the entry itself must exist.

### 5. Present Results

```
## Review Summary

**Assessment**: [APPROVE / REQUEST CHANGES]

### Positive Findings
- What was done well

### Required Changes
- Issues that must be fixed (file:line references)

### Suggestions
- Optional improvements

### Convention Violations
- Any CLAUDE.md or guideline violations
```

Be specific with file:line references. Prioritize correctness and readability over micro-optimizations. If approved, suggest running `/validate-smol` next.

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher

## Arguments (optional, free-form)

Parse these out of the user's invocation text:

- **`final`** — this is the final round of changes. Skip posting `/gemini review` AND skip launching the re-review watcher. Use when the user says things like "last round," "final," "no more reviews needed," "we're done," or explicitly passes `final` to the skill.
- **A PR number** (e.g. `87`) — fetch that specific PR rather than the one matching the current branch. The watcher callback hands this back when a new review arrives on a PR whose branch isn't currently checked out.
- **`--sonnet`** — opt out of the Opus default and run the review pass faster on Sonnet. Use this only when the workflow has been actively reliable (no API errors disrupting the watcher) and the comments look mechanical. Reasoning: Opus is the default because the watcher is meant to enable autonomous review loops; an API error that interrupts the watcher costs more time than Opus's slower output. Sonnet is fine for short, mechanical-decision rounds — but only if the parent session can actually dispatch a Sonnet variant. If you're not sure, leave the default.

Both are optional. Default: current-branch PR, non-final, Opus.

## Fetch pass

Run the helper script to fetch all PR comments since the latest commit:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/fetch_pr_comments.sh [PR_NUMBER]
```

Then process the output:

1. Parse the issue comments, review comments, review summaries, and review threads from the script output.
2. Itemize the list of comments in descending order, with the highest priority comments first.
3. For each comment, include its author and assess if it is a valid comment. If it is valid, provide a detailed description of how you would address the comment.

## After addressing the comments

Automated reviewers (Gemini Code Assist, Copilot, etc.) do not read the conversation and will keep re-surfacing the same concerns every review pass if the threads stay open. To prevent duplicate flags across rounds:

**After you have decided on every bot comment — whether applied or declined — close out the loop for each one.**

For each review comment from `gemini-code-assist[bot]` or `github-copilot` (or any bot author):

1. **Find the matching thread** in the `--- REVIEW THREADS ---` section of the script output by cross-referencing `comment_ids` with the comment `id` you processed. You need the `thread_id`.

2. **Post a short reply** to the thread explaining the decision. Use `gh api` against the PR's comments endpoint. For inline review comments, reply via REST:

   ```bash
   gh api -X POST \
     "repos/${OWNER}/${REPO}/pulls/${PR_NUMBER}/comments/${COMMENT_ID}/replies" \
     -f body="Applied in <SHORT_SHA>." \
     # or:
     -f body="Declined — <one-line rationale, e.g. 'fallback pattern, against project CLAUDE.md'>."
   ```

3. **Resolve the thread** via GraphQL (REST can't do this):

   ```bash
   gh api graphql -f query='
   mutation($id: ID!) {
     resolveReviewThread(input: { threadId: $id }) {
       thread { id isResolved }
     }
   }' -F id="${THREAD_ID}"
   ```

Do this for **every bot comment you addressed**, even the ones you declined. Leaving a declined-with-rationale reply on a resolved thread is what stops the same comment from coming back next round.

Human comments should NOT be auto-resolved — leave those for the human reviewer to resolve after they've seen your response.

## Before pushing fixes: run the project's pre-merge test gate

If you committed any code changes addressing review comments, run the project's full pre-merge gate **before pushing**. The Gemini bot can flag style and surface-level issues, but it does not run tests — a fix that breaks Elixir/GCS/cross-language tests will sail through the bot review only to surface days later when someone runs the full suite.

For smol: `just ci`. Read `feedback_run_full_ci_gate` in memory for why subset commands like `just test-native` are insufficient.

For other projects: look for `just ci`, a `validate-*` skill in `.claude/skills/`, or the canonical command in CLAUDE.md.

If the gate fails:
- Investigate every failure — even ones that look pre-existing. Verify with `git log -- <failing-file>` and `git show <commit>`.
- A failure caused by your fix: fix it before pushing.
- A failure pre-existing on the branch base: name the commit that introduced it, decide between fixing in this PR vs. filing a backlog entry, and tell the user explicitly.

Skip the gate ONLY if you pushed no code changes (declined-only round, replies + thread resolutions only).

## After pushing fixes: re-invoke Gemini

If you pushed new commits to address review comments, post a `/gemini review` comment on the PR so Gemini Code Assist re-runs against the latest commits. Without this, the bot only reviews the original push and your fixes stay unverified.

```bash
gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "/gemini review"
```

Do this **once per PR, after all fixes are pushed**. Not per-thread — one comment triggers a full re-review.

Skip this step if any of these hold:
- You pushed no new commits (e.g., all bot comments were declined with replies, no code changed).
- User passed `final` — this is the last round, no more reviews needed.

## Auto-resume on next Gemini review

When you expect another Gemini review (commits pushed + `/gemini review` posted + not `final`), launch a background watcher per PR and let me auto-resume when it fires. This closes the loop without the user having to tell me "Gemini just posted."

1. **Launch the watcher in the background** for each PR touched:

   ```bash
   ~/.claude/skills/cp-pr-fetcher/scripts/watch_pr_reviews.sh ${PR_NUMBER}
   ```

   Invoke via `Bash(run_in_background=true)`. Capture the returned `shell_id`. The script polls every 30 s, exits 0 on the first new bot review, times out after 2 hours.

2. **Stream it with `Monitor`** on the returned shell_id. Each line of stdout is a notification you'll receive:
   - `WATCHING pr=N baseline=...` — initial setup line, ignore.
   - `NEW_REVIEW pr=N submitted_at=... state=...` — fire signal: a fresh Gemini review has landed.
   - `TIMEOUT pr=N ...` (stderr) — 2-hour cap hit; tell the user and stop.

3. **When a `NEW_REVIEW` arrives**, auto-re-invoke this skill scoped to that PR:

   ```
   Skill(cp-pr-fetcher, args="${PR_NUMBER}")
   ```

   The skill handles its own fetch → address → push → re-trigger → watch cycle from there. Multiple PRs touched in one pass → one watcher per PR, process in whatever order they fire.

Skip the watcher if:
- You skipped the `/gemini review` step above for any reason (no commits, or `final`).
- The user explicitly told you not to wait for another review.

## Guidance

- Keep replies terse. "Applied in abc1234." or "Declined — useMemo is redundant under React Compiler." is enough.
- If you applied a fix, reference the commit SHA so the thread records the connection.
- If you declined, give the one-line reason that cites the project convention or doc (e.g. "CLAUDE.md: no fallbacks", "spec: reducer is the validation point").
- Batch the reply + resolve after you have finished the itemized analysis. Do not intersperse — finish the review pass, then close the loop in one sweep.

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/skills/cp-plan

# cp-plan

Multi-phase planning skill that turns a design spec into a sequence of small, independently reviewable PRs — and proves the decomposition works by dry-running the test phase against the planned types before any code ships.

## When to use

- A brainstorming design spec exists at `docs/superpowers/specs/...-design.md`.
- The work would otherwise become one large feature branch (>10 files, multiple distinct concerns).
- You want review checkpoints between chunks of work, not just at the end.

Do NOT use cp-plan for:

- Pure bug fixes touching a single file or a single mechanical refactor sweep — go straight to `superpowers:writing-plans`.
- Greenfield work without a brainstorming spec — run `/superpowers:brainstorming` first.
- Per-PR step-by-step planning — that's `superpowers:writing-plans`'s job, invoked from `--refine`.
- **Small features.** If the total scope is ≤10 files across ≤2 distinct concerns, cp-plan's multi-phase ceremony has more overhead than benefit — go straight to `superpowers:writing-plans`. Heuristic: if you can't think of three distinct PRs that each have a different reviewer-question, the work is too small.

### Special case: non-executable deliverables

For markdown skills, configuration, prompts, or any deliverable without a unit-test framework (GTest, pytest, ExUnit), the **test phase is degenerate** — there is no `DISABLED_` marker mechanism to apply. In these cases:

- Replace the test-phase pseudocode section with a **behavioral spec** describing expected behavior in plain prose: "given X, when invoked with Y, the deliverable produces Z."
- The dogfood run *is* the test. Capture observed-vs-predicted behavior in the refinement log.
- The size threshold above usually rules out cp-plan for these deliverables anyway. If you find yourself running cp-plan on a markdown-only spec, double-check whether `superpowers:writing-plans` would serve better.

## Modes

### Initial mode: `/cp-plan <spec-path>`

Decompose a fresh design spec into a master plan. Output: one markdown file at `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature>-cp-plan.md` based on the template at `~/.claude/skills/cp-plan/templates/cp-plan-template.md`.

### Refinement mode: `/cp-plan --refine <PR#|slug>`

Once a planned PR's dependencies have merged, refine that single PR into an executable plan via `superpowers:writing-plans`. Append a refinement-log entry to the master plan file.

### Status mode: `/cp-plan --status`

Read the master plan file. For each PR in the sequence table, query git log for a commit subject matching the title slug. Update the `Status` column to `merged` for matches.

## Initial mode flow

1. **Read the design spec** in full. Extract: feature name, the units of work it implies (modules, classes, integrations), and any explicit non-goals.

2. **Read the template** at `~/.claude/skills/cp-plan/templates/cp-plan-template.md`. Use as the structural skeleton.

3. **Decompose into PRs** following this canonical order:
   - **PR 1: Types and interfaces.** All new structs, classes (declarations only), enums, function signatures, build wiring for new compilation units. No bodies. No tests. Builds green.
   - **PR 2: Integration wiring.** Plumb the new types through existing modules — slow-loop registration, queue declarations, builder injection, parameter store entries, etc. No business logic. Builds green.
   - **PR 3: Test contract.** Test cases asserting expected behavior, marked `DISABLED_` (GTest), `@pytest.mark.skip(reason="awaiting <PR# or feature>")` (pytest), or `@tag :pending` (ExUnit). Tests are real code that compiles, but the markers keep CI green.
   - **PR 4..N: Implementation chunks.** Each chunk turns a coherent subset of the disabled tests green. Target ≤10 files per chunk; explicitly label refactor-sweep PRs with one of: `rename`, `move`, `type-rename`, `signature-prop`, `format-only`.

   Splitting heuristics:
   - If two tests would share the same impl change, they belong in one chunk.
   - If a chunk would touch >10 non-refactor files, split by responsibility (not by line-count).
   - If a single PR is doing two distinct things, split.

4. **Plan PR 1 at executable detail** in the master plan file: every type, every signature, every file path, every build wiring change.

5. **Plan PRs 2..N as summaries only.** Title, file list (target ≤10), 2-4 sentence "what" description. No executable detail. The user (or future cp-plan invocations) will run `/cp-plan --refine <PR#>` after dependency PRs merge to spawn writing-plans-format executable plans.

6. **Write the test-phase dry-run** — pseudocode test cases for the test-contract PR, using PR 1 types verbatim. Include the `DISABLED_` (or skip / pending) markers in the pseudocode so it copy-pastes into the real test files. Aim for completeness: every behavior the impl chunks must satisfy lives in this section.

7. **Write the impl-phase dry-run** — narrative prose per impl PR. Which disabled tests it enables, files it touches, why this chunk.

8. **Run the dry-run self-check.** Answer the three questions inline:
   - Can every test be written using only PR 1 types?
   - Can every impl chunk be done in ≤10 files (except labeled refactor sweeps)?
   - Is any PR doing two things?

   If any answer is "no," **iterate** — fix the earlier section, re-answer. Do not write the file with "no" answers; the dry-run is the gate, not a checkbox.

9. **Write the master plan file** to `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature>-cp-plan.md`.

10. **Commit the master plan file** on the current branch with subject `docs(plan): cp-plan for <feature>`. Do NOT push (per project rule).

## Refinement mode flow (`--refine <PR#|slug>`)

1. Locate the master plan file (most recent `*-cp-plan.md` in `docs/superpowers/plans/` unless ambiguous — if ambiguous, ask).
2. Find the named PR in the sequence table. Verify all rows in the `Depends on` column for this PR have `Status = merged`. If not, refuse and report which dependency is unmerged.
3. Invoke `superpowers:writing-plans` with two inputs: the master plan file and the specific PR's row + summary block. The output goes to `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature>-pr<N>-<slug>-plan.md`.
4. Append a refinement log entry to the master plan file:

```markdown
### {{YYYY-MM-DD}} — PR {{N}} refined
- Spawned plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/.../<spawned>.md`
- Drift from dry-run: {{describe any reality-vs-prediction differences, or "none"}}
```

   The "drift" entry is required even if "none" — empty drift entries are signal that the dry-run is predictive.

5. Update the row's `Status` to `refined`.
6. Commit the master plan update with subject `docs(plan): refine PR <N> (<slug>)`.

## Status mode flow (`--status`)

1. Read the master plan file.
2. For each PR row with `Status = planned` or `refined`, run:
   ```bash
   git log --oneline main..HEAD --grep="<slug>"
   ```
   If a commit subject contains the slug, set `Status = merged`.
3. If any row's status changed, write the updated file. If nothing changed, report "no changes."

This mode uses commit-subject heuristics. Slugs may collide; if a row's "merged" detection looks wrong, report it and ask.

## Output discipline

- **Use feature-meaningful PR titles.** "MissionPlanner types and PlannedLeg struct," not "Phase 1." The internal phase concept stays internal.
- **The dry-run pseudocode is real code in pseudocode form.** Use real types from PR 1, real method signatures, real assertion macros. The test PR will copy-paste this with minor edits.
- **The refinement log is append-only.** Past entries are retrospective material; never delete.

## What cp-plan does not do

- Does not run brainstorming. The spec is an input.
- Does not run any executable detail planning itself — `--refine` invokes `superpowers:writing-plans`.
- Does not implement code. cp-plan emits plan artifacts only.
- Does not push commits. Per project rule, all commits stay local until the user requests `git push`.

## Required reading before invoking

- The design spec at the path passed in.
- `~/.claude/skills/cp-plan/templates/cp-plan-template.md`.
- `~/.claude/skills/code-writing-guidelines/SKILL.md` (for the "Plan in three layers" + "Validate by dry-running tests" sections).
- The smol repo's CLAUDE.md (for project-specific naming and style conventions referenced in the plan).


ARGUMENTS: --refine 8

---

Base directory for this skill: /home/ubuntu/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans — one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## No Placeholders

Every step must contain the actual content an engineer needs. These are **plan failures** — never write them:
- "TBD", "TODO", "implement later", "fill in details"
- "Add appropriate error handling" / "add validation" / "handle edge cases"
- "Write tests for the above" (without actual test code)
- "Similar to Task N" (repeat the code — the engineer may be reading tasks out of order)
- Steps that describe what to do without showing how (code blocks required for code steps)
- References to types, functions, or methods not defined in any task

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in every step — if a step changes code, show the code
- Exact commands with expected output
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Self-Review

After writing the complete plan, look at the spec with fresh eyes and check the plan against it. This is a checklist you run yourself — not a subagent dispatch.

**1. Spec coverage:** Skim each section/requirement in the spec. Can you point to a task that implements it? List any gaps.

**2. Placeholder scan:** Search your plan for red flags — any of the patterns from the "No Placeholders" section above. Fix them.

**3. Type consistency:** Do the types, method signatures, and property names you used in later tasks match what you defined in earlier tasks? A function called `clearLayers()` in Task 3 but `clearFullLayers()` in Task 7 is a bug.

If you find issues, fix them inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on. If you find a spec requirement with no task, add the task.

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review


ARGUMENTS: Refine PR 8a from the master cp-plan at `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-27-mission-planner-restoration-cp-plan.md` into an executable plan.

**Output file:** `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-27-mission-planner-restoration-pr8a-fly-to-waypoint-skill-plan.md`

**Branch suggestion:** `feat/pr8a-fly-to-waypoint`

**PR 8a goal:** Create the `fly_to_waypoint` skill scaffolding — header, implementation, ActionId, registry registration, and a per-skill GTest binary with active (non-DISABLED) tests pinning the contract. The skill is reachable only via test harness in 8a; PR 8b builds the Phase 3 demo graph that dispatches to it at runtime.

**Reviewer-question for PR 8a:** "is `fly_to_waypoint` correctly implemented as a skill consuming `ctx.current_planned_leg`?"

**Sequence-table row** (from line 25 of the master plan after the split): `| 8a | fly_to_waypoint skill scaffolding (skill + ActionId + registry + per-skill tests) | 7 | PR 0–7c | no | planned |`

**Refinement-log context for PR 8** (lines 549-560 of master plan): the master plan's original PR 8 description was based on a wrong premise (assumed `fly_to_waypoint.cpp` existed). It does not — only `idle`, `loiter_at_point`, `take_off` are in `src/mission/skills/`. PR 8 was split 2026-04-29 into 8a (skill scaffolding) + 8b (graph wiring + integration tests).

DRIFT FROM MASTER PLAN PSEUDOCODE that the writing-plans pass MUST capture:

1. **Tests are ACTIVE, not DISABLED.** The master plan's PR 8 pseudocode at lines 313-338 uses `TEST(FlyToWaypoint, DISABLED_UsesPlannedLegFromContext)` etc., assuming PR 5 had landed those as DISABLED. Per PR 5's drift entry, the FlyToWaypoint DISABLED tests were excluded. PR 8a writes them fresh, ACTIVE (no `DISABLED_` prefix).

2. **Pseudocode pointer-projection bugs.** Master plan line 319 says `ctx.current_planned_leg = MakePlannedLegStub(...)` — but `SkillContext::current_planned_leg` is `const PlannedLeg*` (PR 4 round 3 changed it). Tests must do `PlannedLeg leg = MakePlannedLegStub(2); ctx.current_planned_leg = &leg;`. Line 331 `ctx.current_planned_leg = std::nullopt;` — must instead be `ctx.current_planned_leg = nullptr;` (it's a pointer, not optional).

3. **Cmd.primary IS std::optional<PlannedLeg>** (the owning storage on `SkillCommand`, distinct from the non-owning view on `SkillContext`). So `EXPECT_TRUE(cmd.primary.has_value())` and `cmd.primary->leg_index` are correct as-written.

4. **fly_to_waypoint API shape**: follow the `take_off` namespace pattern (`namespace fly_to_waypoint { void Run(...); }`). Loiter uses a free function `LoiterAtPoint` — the namespace pattern is the newer convention (take_off was added more recently).

5. **Capture detection.** Per the master plan's PR 6 description (line 208) "capture detection (`HorizontalDistance`, `kWaypointCaptureRadiusM`, altitude tolerance) is unchanged" — but since this is fresh code, the constants need to be defined here. Reference `kWaypointCaptureRadiusM` on main: `grep -rn "kWaypointCaptureRadiusM\|kWaypointAltitudeToleranceM" src/`. If they don't exist, define them in `fly_to_waypoint.cpp` as `inline constexpr` per `feedback_no_tunable_thresholds`. Use `dubins::Waypoint`'s `north_m`/`east_m`/`altitude_m` fields plus the SkillContext's NED position.

6. **WorldProgress side-effects on capture.** Per the cp-plan summary at line 208, capture sets `world.waypoint.action_pending = true` and stamps `world.waypoint.reached_tick_ms`. Verify `WorldProgress::WaypointProgress` has those fields on current main.

7. **Naming conventions discovered post-7c:** project ratified `_altitude_m` = MSL (no suffix), `_height_agl_m` = AGL. `dubins::Waypoint.altitude_m` is MSL. Don't introduce any `_agl_m` or `_msl_m` field unless it's a distinct frame. Capture-radius constant: `kWaypointCaptureRadiusM` (no frame suffix, distance is a horizontal scalar).

8. **Per `feedback_no_default_arguments` (CLAUDE.md):** `fly_to_waypoint::Run` takes `(const SkillContext&, WorldProgress*, SkillCommand*)`. No default arguments anywhere.

9. **Test fixture pattern:** copy from `test/test_take_off/test_take_off.cpp` (newer pattern, post-7c). `MakeBaseSkillContext()` is mentioned in the master plan but doesn't actually exist on main — verify with `grep -rn "MakeBaseSkillContext" test/` and either reuse the local builder pattern from test_take_off or define it in this test file.

10. **Per `feedback_apply_clear_resolutions`:** when the cp-plan summary points at a clear resolution path (e.g., copy capture-detection from a doc reference), apply directly without re-deciding.

CRITICAL FILES TO VERIFY AGAINST CURRENT MAIN (post-PR-7c at 4cc24570):

- `src/mission/skill_context.h` — confirm `const PlannedLeg* current_planned_leg` field exists.
- `src/mission/world_progress.h` — confirm `WaypointProgress { current_index, action_pending, reached_tick_ms, ... }`.
- `src/mission/action_id.h` — confirm `kActionTakeOff`, `kRegisteredActionIds` size 3, `ActionIdsAreUnique()`.
- `src/mission/skill_registry.h` — confirm 3-entry array shape.
- `src/mission/skills/take_off.h` and `.cpp` — copy as the structural template.
- `test/test_take_off/test_take_off.cpp` — copy as the test-fixture template.
- `src/core/skill_command.h` — confirm `SkillCommand` has `std::optional<PlannedLeg> primary`.
- `src/navigation/mission_planner.h` — confirm `PlannedLeg` struct shape (leg_index, leg, etc.).
- `lib/dubins/dubins/planner.h` — confirm `dubins::Waypoint` shape (north_m, east_m, altitude_m, heading_deg).

EXISTING TEST DIRS RELEVANT TO PR 8a:

- `test/test_take_off/` — exists, structural template.
- `test/test_loiter_at_point/` — exists, alternative template (older pattern).
- `test/test_fly_to_waypoint/` — DOES NOT EXIST, CREATE in PR 8a.

CMake registration: `test/CMakeLists.txt` uses GLOB; `touch` it after creating the new test directory to trigger reconfigure. Walk the writer through that step.

THE PR 8a PLAN MUST:

- Specify exactly the files PR 8a will touch (target ≤7 per the sequence-table estimate). Surgical reasoning per file.
- Walk through fly_to_waypoint::Run body: read `ctx.current_planned_leg`, write `cmd->primary` if non-null, run capture detection, mutate `world->waypoint` on capture.
- Include the active test bodies adapted to the real API (pointer-projection patterns, no DISABLED prefix, fixture in same .cpp file).
- CMake registration via `touch test/CMakeLists.txt`.
- Verify `just ci` (the project's full pre-merge gate per `feedback_run_full_ci_gate`) at the end.
- Include the writing-plans self-review pass.
- One-commit-per-task discipline.

AFTER PRODUCING THE PLAN: commit with subject `docs(plan): refine PR 8a (fly_to_waypoint-skill)` per the cp-plan refinement flow. Do NOT push. The cp-plan refinement step that follows the writing-plans output will append a refinement-log entry to the master plan documenting any drift discovered during refinement and flip PR 8a's row from `planned` → `refined`.

Branch base: current `main` HEAD `42799d74` (after the PR 8 split commit). Expected branch name: `feat/pr8a-fly-to-waypoint`.
